The breadcrumb row is a single-line strip occupying the full content width but visually reading as a slim horizontal trail along the left, with generous 24–32px top margin from the header and about 16–24px bottom margin before the demo header panel. Its vertical height is compact (around 32–40px), with 8–12px internal padding above and below the breadcrumb text, keeping it light and unobtrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as part of the standard content flow; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The demo header spans almost the entire width of the centered content container, positioned directly beneath the breadcrumb navigation with a comfortable 16–24px vertical gap. It reads as the hero/meta panel: a horizontally oriented, rounded rectangle that stretches from the left margin of the content band to the right, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Within this panel, the demo title is visually centered in the upper half, echoing the prominence of the script ‘Acting’ heading, while supporting metadata (voice actor name, category, length, client, etc.) is aligned slightly left of center or arranged in a neat two-column grid beneath the title. The panel’s height is moderate—around 20–25% of the viewport height on desktop—with plenty of whitespace above and below the text, giving it a theatrical, hero-like presence without being heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll; acts as the top anchor of the main detail content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The demo tags panel is placed immediately beneath the demo header, separated by a 12–16px vertical gap to keep them visually grouped but distinct. It occupies the full width of the central content container, with its content left-aligned starting at the same column as the breadcrumb. Tags are displayed as small pill-shaped chips arranged in a horizontal row that can wrap onto a second line if necessary. Vertical height is relatively compact—16–24px tall per tag row with 8–12px top and bottom padding in the panel area—so the entire tag strip feels like a light, breathable metadata band spanning the page, acting as a visual bridge between the hero header above and the heavier two-column section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main vertical flow, with tags wrapping responsively as the viewport narrows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The audio player sits at the top of the right-hand column in the main two-column layout, which starts after a generous 32–40px gap below the tags panel. The right column occupies roughly 60%–65% of the central content width (about 7 out of 12 grid columns), aligned to the right, with a 32–40px horizontal gap separating it from the left column. The audio player itself is a wide, pill-style component stretching the full width of this right column, with substantial horizontal padding (16–24px) and vertical padding (12–16px) to mirror the pill-like audio rows from the mockup. It is the first element in the right column and sits slightly higher than the top of the left-column card, reinforcing its primary focus in the visual hierarchy. There is about 16–24px of vertical space beneath the player before any additional controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, maintaining its position at the top of that stack; on smaller screens, it becomes the first major element after tags when the columns stack vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating summary occupies the upper portion of the left column in the main two-column section. The left column takes about 35%–40% of the content width (roughly 5 out of 12 grid columns) and is aligned with the left edge of the container. The rating summary is presented as a compact card with rounded corners, filling nearly the full width of the left column, with 16–24px internal padding. It aligns vertically so that its top edge is roughly level with, or slightly below, the top edge of the audio player across the gap, producing a harmonious horizontal band between audio and rating. The card’s height is moderate, enough to comfortably display an average rating, star visualization, and a small breakdown, but it leaves substantial vertical space below for the review list card. A 16–24px vertical gap separates the rating summary from the recent reviews list beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the left column stack; on narrow screens, it appears below the audio player and primary actions when columns collapse.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent reviews list sits directly beneath the rating summary within the left column, separated by a consistent 16–24px vertical gap. It fills the remaining height of the left column content area, forming a taller card-like section that mirrors the vertical presence of the image in the original mockup. This card also uses a rounded rectangle style, with 16–24px internal padding and a white or very light background, visually distinct yet harmonious with the rating summary above. Reviews are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between each review item, allowing a clear, scannable list from top to bottom. The card stretches nearly the full height of the main content band, so when viewed alongside the right column’s stack, it forms a balanced, two-column composition where the left pillar is primarily textual and evaluative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the left column; as the page scrolls, this list extends downward, and on small screens it moves below the core player/actions as part of a single column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites control is positioned in the right column directly beneath the audio player, within the same visual group of primary actions. It appears as a compact icon-plus-label or toggle button aligned to the left edge of the right column, sitting in a horizontal row of controls. This row occupies the full width of the right column but remains relatively shallow in height (around 40–48px), with 8–12px of vertical padding. The favorites control typically appears as the leftmost element in this row, with a 16–24px horizontal gap separating it from the project shortlist control to its right. There is a modest 12–16px vertical gap between the bottom of the audio player and the top of this control row, maintaining the airy feel while clearly linking them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored beneath the audio player; on smaller screens, it remains grouped directly under the player, stacking above or beside the shortlist control depending on available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project shortlist control sits in the same horizontal control row as the favorites control, beneath the audio player in the right column. It is positioned immediately to the right of the favorites control, with a consistent 16–24px horizontal gap between them, and shares the same vertical alignment and height. The control is sized similarly (around 40–48px tall) and maintains equal left/right padding for visual balance. This row may extend slightly toward the center of the right column, stopping short of the right edge to preserve white space. The project shortlist control is thus prominent but subordinate to the audio player, visually recognized as a direct action associated with the current demo.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control row; on narrow screens, it may drop to a new line below the favorites control, but remains immediately associated with the player.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project shortlist mini-panel is placed slightly lower in the right column, functioning like a compact summary card. It typically appears beneath the project plan CTA, offset toward the right side of the column to echo a small floating panel without detaching from the grid. Vertically, there is a 16–24px gap between the CTA and the top of this mini-panel. Horizontally, it occupies about 60–70% of the right column’s width and is right-aligned, leaving a subtle strip of whitespace between it and the main column gap to the left. Its height is modest but flexible, expanding as items are added. This positioning ensures it is easy to find when looking to the lower-right of the main interaction area, acting as a running summary of what’s been shortlisted while the user reviews and listens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Generally static within the right column but can optionally use a sticky behavior on taller viewports—remaining visible within the right column as the user scrolls through longer review content—while still respecting the overall page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project plan CTA is the strongest secondary action in the right column and is located directly beneath the control row containing favorites and shortlist. It appears as a full-width, high-contrast button that spans nearly the entire width of the right column, with 16–20px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding to feel substantial but not oversized. A 16–24px vertical gap separates it from the controls row above, and another 16–24px gap separates it from the shortlist mini-panel below. Its alignment is to the left edge of the right column, matching the audio player and controls, ensuring a clean vertical spine of interactive elements. In the overall composition, it sits roughly mid-height in the two-column field, easily reachable after interacting with the player and controls, and visually balanced against the taller review block on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; on smaller screens it remains directly below the audio player actions, maintaining its role as the primary follow-up action after listening.

name: Acting
title: Acting
description: Page for viewing acting showreels, filtering and sorting them, viewing overview information about on-camera work, and building a casting shortlist.

core_features: Display acting content in two tabs: an Overview tab with descriptive HTML content and a Showreels tab listing acting showreels.
Load Overview tab content using getActingPageContent, including the default tab identifier used when no tab URL parameter is provided.
Provide a Showreels filter and sort bar populated dynamically via getActingFilterOptions (genres, year range, duration range, and sort options).
Search and paginate showreels through searchShowreels using the current filters, sort order, and page number derived from URL parameters.
Display showreels in a grid or list with thumbnail, title, metadata, and description snippet; clicking opens showreel_detail.html?id={showreelId}.
Allow optional inline playback of showreels in the grid using each item's video_url field from searchShowreels.
Allow adding showreels to a Casting List with addShowreelToCastingList and show an up-to-date casting summary via getCastingList.
Enable navigation to project_planner.html with a prefilled title parameter from both Overview and Showreels tabs.
Enable navigation to rates.html from links related to on-camera work rates.
user_workflows: Browse acting showreels from main navigation: 1) User clicks the global 'Acting' nav link. 2) Page parses incoming URL parameters (tab, genre, min_year, max_year, min_duration_seconds, max_duration_seconds, sort_by, page). 3) Page calls getActingPageContent to obtain overview_html and default_tab. 4) If no tab parameter is present, the page activates default_tab from getActingPageContent; when coming from main navigation, this default will be set to the Showreels tab. 5) Page calls getActingFilterOptions to build filter controls. 6) Page calls searchShowreels with filters derived from URL parameters and a configured page_size. 7) Page calls getCastingList to show any existing casting shortlist. 8) User sees the Showreels tab active with filterable, sortable showreels.

Filter and sort showreels: 1) On the Showreels tab, the user changes a filter (e.g., selects a genre, adjusts year range, or sets a minimum/maximum duration) using options from getActingFilterOptions. 2) The filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters (genre, min_year, max_year, min_duration_seconds, max_duration_seconds) and resets page to 1. 3) The page re-invokes searchShowreels with the updated parameters and the current sort_by. 4) When the user changes the sort order (choosing one of sort_options.id values), the sort_by URL parameter is updated and searchShowreels is called again. 5) The showreel grid updates to reflect new filters and sort order without reloading the entire page beyond the necessary data.

Paginate showreels: 1) After a searchShowreels response is loaded, pagination controls compute available pages from total_results and page_size. 2) When the user selects a different page (e.g., clicking a page number, next, or previous), the page URL parameter is updated to that page number while keeping all other parameters. 3) searchShowreels is called again with the new page value. 4) The showreel grid scrolls to the top of results (if implemented) and displays showreels for that page. 5) If the user copies the URL or returns to this page, the same page of results is restored based on the page parameter.

Play inline showreels and view details: 1) In the showreel grid, each item has a play action that uses the item's video_url (from searchShowreels) for inline playback. 2) When the user clicks play, the grid component optionally pauses other active players and starts playback for the selected item. 3) When the user clicks the showreel's thumbnail or title instead, the page navigates to showreel_detail.html with the id URL parameter set to that item's showreel id, preserving the current acting page URL (including filters, sort_by, and page) in the browser history. 4) After viewing a detail and using the browser Back button or a 'Back to Acting' link that points to acting.html with the previous URL parameters, the user is returned to the same filtered, sorted, and paged view.

Build and review a casting list: 1) When the Acting page loads, it calls getCastingList to determine if a Casting List exists and to show its current items and count. 2) In the showreel grid, each item displays its current is_in_casting_list state from searchShowreels to indicate whether it is already shortlisted. 3) When the user clicks an 'Add to casting list' action on a showreel, addShowreelToCastingList is called with that showreelId. 4) On success, the casting list summary component updates from the addShowreelToCastingList response.casting_list (including total_items and items) and the corresponding showreel item's is_in_casting_list state is updated in the UI. 5) The casting list summary component allows the user to review all shortlisted showreels (titles, genres, years, durations) using data from getCastingList or from the latest addShowreelToCastingList response. 6) Clicking a showreel in the casting summary navigates to showreel_detail.html?id={showreel_id}.

Use Overview content and plan an acting project: 1) User switches to the Overview tab using the tab control (if not already on it). 2) The Overview tab displays overview_html from getActingPageContent. 3) Within the overview content area or associated actions, the user can click a 'Plan an acting project' link/button. 4) The page navigates to project_planner.html with a title URL parameter (e.g., set to an acting-related project title as defined by navigation rules). 5) From the Overview or Showreels tab, the user can also click a 'Discuss rates for on-camera work' link, which navigates to rates.html without additional parameters.

Deep linking and external access for casting teams: 1) A casting team member receives or copies a URL like acting.html with query parameters (e.g., tab, genre, min_year, max_year, min_duration_seconds, max_duration_seconds, sort_by, page). 2) When they open this URL, the page initializes using those parameters directly, bypassing any internal defaults. 3) getActingFilterOptions populates filters so that the UI reflects the current URL-based filter and sort state (e.g., the correct genre and ranges selected). 4) searchShowreels uses the URL parameters to load the matching set of showreels. 5) The casting team can then adjust filters, change sorting, or add selected showreels to a Casting List using addShowreelToCastingList, with any changes reflected in the URL and casting summary.
interactions: Tab switching: Clicking the 'Overview' tab sets tab=overview in the URL and shows the Overview panel; clicking the 'Showreels' tab sets tab=showreels and shows the Showreels panel. The tab state is always derived from the URL parameter.

Initial content load: On page load, a controller component reads URL parameters, calls getActingPageContent to obtain overview_html and default_tab, then decides which tab to activate when tab is missing, calls getActingFilterOptions to configure filters, calls searchShowreels when the Showreels tab is active, and calls getCastingList to populate the casting summary.

Filter changes: Adjusting genre, year range, or duration controls updates the corresponding URL parameters (genre, min_year, max_year, min_duration_seconds, max_duration_seconds), resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchShowreels call. The filter UI syncs its selected values from the URL on every load.
Sort changes: Selecting a sort option from a dropdown sets sort_by in the URL to the selected sort_options.id and triggers a new searchShowreels call with the updated sort_by value.
Pagination controls: Clicking pagination controls updates page in the URL and triggers searchShowreels with the new page while preserving other filter/sort parameters.

Showreel item click: Clicking a showreel thumbnail or title navigates to showreel_detail.html with id set to that showreel's id. The Acting page's current URL (including filters, sort, and page) remains in browser history so Back navigation restores the same state.

Inline playback: Clicking a 'Play' control on a showreel item starts inline playback using video_url from the searchShowreels result for that item. Clicking 'Pause' or interacting with another player stops the current playback as per component logic.

Add to casting list: Clicking an 'Add to casting list' or similar shortlist action on a showreel invokes addShowreelToCastingList with that showreelId. On success, the component updates the local representation of is_in_casting_list for that showreel and refreshes the casting summary using data from the response.casting_list.

Casting list summary interactions: On load or after any add operation, getCastingList or addShowreelToCastingList.casting_list drives the summary UI. Clicking a showreel in the casting summary navigates to showreel_detail.html?id={showreel_id}.
Project planner link: Clicking 'Plan an acting project' in either tab navigates to project_planner.html with a title parameter set as specified in outgoing navigation rules.
Rates link: Clicking 'Discuss rates for on-camera work' navigates to rates.html.
URL sharing: When a user copies the current browser URL and shares it, another user opening that URL will land on the same tab, with the same filters, sort order, and page applied via URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL parameters and API responses. On load, the page reads tab, genre, min_year, max_year, min_duration_seconds, max_duration_seconds, sort_by, and page from the URL. It then calls getActingPageContent; if tab is not present in the URL, the page uses the default_tab value returned from getActingPageContent as the active tab. If the active tab is 'showreels', getActingFilterOptions is called to obtain available genres, year_range, duration_range, and sort_options. The filter component interprets current URL parameters and sets its UI accordingly. The searchShowreels request is constructed using the current URL parameters for genre, min_year, max_year, min_duration_seconds, max_duration_seconds, sort_by, plus page and a page_size defined by layout or configuration. The response's showreels array, along with pagination metadata (total_results, page, page_size), controls the Showreels grid and pagination controls. The is_in_casting_list field for each showreel is used to render shortlist indicators. The page calls getCastingList on load to obtain any existing casting_list and its items, which drives the casting summary UI. When the user changes filters, sorting, pagination, or tab selection, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters first and then re-fetches data as needed. No persistent client-side storage such as localStorage is used; all restorable state is encoded in the URL and reconstructed via getActingPageContent, getActingFilterOptions, searchShowreels, addShowreelToCastingList, and getCastingList on each load or navigation.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load and responds to URL changes by selecting the active tab, fetching overview content, configuring filters, loading showreels, and loading the casting list.
Provides controls to switch between the Overview and Showreels tabs and ensures the selected tab is reflected in and derived from the tab URL parameter.
Displays static and semi-static overview HTML for acting content and contains navigation links to the Project Planner and Rates pages.

Displays filter and sort controls for showreels, including genre selection, year range, duration range, and sorting options, based entirely on options returned from getActingFilterOptions and current URL parameters.

Renders a grid or list of showreels based on the current searchShowreels response, including thumbnail, title, genre label, year filmed, display duration, description snippet, accent, age_range, notable_credits, and current is_in_casting_list state.
Displays pagination controls for the showreel results using total_results, page, and page_size from searchShowreels, and updates the page URL parameter when the user navigates between pages.
Provides inline playback controls for each showreel using video_url from searchShowreels and coordinates playback so only the intended showreel plays at a time.
Displays the current Casting List if it exists, including total_items and each shortlisted showreel's key attributes, and allows navigation to showreel_detail.html for each item.
Encapsulates the logic for adding a specific showreel to the Casting List by calling addShowreelToCastingList and then notifying other components (such as the grid and casting summary) of the update.
Keeps URL parameters synchronized with UI state for tab selection, filters, sorting, and pagination, and restores that state on page load or when the URL changes.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 2-column layout likely based on a centered max-width container (approx. 1080–1200px) with a simple percentage-based grid. Overall layout could easily map to a 12-column system (image ~5 columns, audio list ~7 columns).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top‑to‑bottom flow: global full‑width black header bar at the very top (outside this component list) → vertical gap → centered main content container controlled by the acting-page-controller. Inside this container, a large, centered page title 'Acting' sits near the top, followed by a horizontal acting-tab-switcher row. Below the tabs, the content splits into a clear two-column layout within a max-width (~1120px) centered band: the left column houses the acting-overview-panel at the top and the casting-list-summary beneath it; the right column houses, from top to bottom, the showreels-filter-bar, then the vertically stacked showreels-results-grid (composed of showreel-inline-player cards with add-to-casting-list-action inside), and finally the showreels-pagination-controls at the bottom. The acting-deeplink-state-sync is a non-visual controller attached logically to the filter/results zone and does not disturb this flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top‑to‑bottom flow: global full‑width black header bar at the very top (outside this component list) → vertical gap → centered main content container controlled by the acting-page-controller. Inside this container, a large, centered page title 'Acting' sits near the top, followed by a horizontal acting-tab-switcher row. Below the tabs, the content splits into a clear two-column layout within a max-width (~1120px) centered band: the left column houses the acting-overview-panel at the top and the casting-list-summary beneath it; the right column houses, from top to bottom, the showreels-filter-bar, then the vertically stacked showreels-results-grid (composed of showreel-inline-player cards with add-to-casting-list-action inside), and finally the showreels-pagination-controls at the bottom. The acting-deeplink-state-sync is a non-visual controller attached logically to the filter/results zone and does not disturb this flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the global header takes roughly 12–15% of the viewport height. The main 'Acting' title plus its top and bottom spacing and the tab row beneath occupy another ~20–25% of the initial viewport, creating a strong hero section. The remaining space is devoted to the two-column content block. Horizontally, all main content is constrained to a centered container with wide left/right margins (about 15–20% of the viewport combined), preserving a comfortable central band. Within this band, the two-column section assigns roughly 40% of the width to the left column and 60% to the right column, echoing the mockup’s image vs. audio-list proportions. Major vertical separations between title, tabs, and the two-column section use 32–48px spacing; gaps between columns and individual components within columns use 16–32px increments based on the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a moderately spacious feel. Each major block (title, tabs, overview panel, filter bar, results grid, shortlist summary) is separated with clear vertical breathing room (24–32px) so nothing feels cramped. The showreel-inline-player cards inside the showreels-results-grid are stacked with consistent spacing (16–24px between rows), similar to the pill-style audio players in the mockup, and they avoid heavy text to maintain a light, scannable density. The casting-list-summary card is compact but still uses comfortable padding (16–24px) and whitespace to avoid feeling like a dense sidebar. Overall, interactions are focused on a small number of key elements per row, avoiding cluttered controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s Z-pattern and then transitions into a strong vertical scan. The eye first moves left-to-right across the global black header bar (logo to navigation), then drops to the centered 'Acting' title. From there, it moves slightly downward to the acting-tab-switcher row, scanning it horizontally. The eye then descends into the two-column section, landing first on the left column’s acting-overview-panel (playing the role of the large hero image). After taking in that focal element, the user’s attention shifts right to the showreels-filter-bar and then flows downward through the stacked showreel-inline-player cards in the showreels-results-grid. The casting-list-summary in the left column provides a secondary vertical anchor that can become sticky as the user scrolls, periodically drawing the eye back left to the shortlist while the right column remains the main vertical scanning track.

layout.overall_description: Below a full-width black header bar, the Acting page opens into a bright white canvas where a large, centered 'Acting' title sits at the top of a wide but clearly bounded content column. Directly underneath, a row of clean, pill-shaped tabs stretches horizontally but stays within the same centered max-width, underlining that this is one section of a cohesive portfolio. A generous vertical gap leads into a two-column band that dominates the rest of the page: on the left, a tall rounded-rectangle overview panel with a portrait-style hero image and key on-camera details anchors the layout visually, echoing the theatrical LEGO-style hero from the mockup. Beneath it, a compact but well-padded casting shortlist card nests into the same column, ready to summarize selected showreels. On the right, aligned to the top edge of the overview panel, sits a slim filter bar with sorting and facet controls, which then flows into a vertical stack of wide, pill-like showreel cards, each functioning like a modern inline media player. These rows fill the right column from top to bottom, separated by consistent white space, with a discrete pagination control aligned to the bottom-right. The whole composition feels airy yet intentional: a black-and-white, theater-inspired interface where a strong central headline, a hero-like overview image on the left, and a clean vertical list of interactive showreels on the right create a clear journey from discovery to selection, while an invisible state-sync layer quietly keeps filters and deep links in sync behind the scenes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The acting-page-controller defines the main content canvas beneath the global black header bar. It spans the full browser width but constrains its child content to a centered max-width container (approximately 1080–1200px) with wide left and right margins (around 15–20% of the viewport combined), preserving the mockup’s central band. Vertically, it begins roughly 32–48px below the header and stretches down to the page’s bottom. Within it, the controller stacks the 'Acting' title at the top, then the acting-tab-switcher, and then the two-column content area. The two-column section is laid out using a 12-column-like grid where the left column occupies about 5/12 of the width and the right column 7/12, with a 24–32px gutter between them. On narrow screens, the controller collapses the layout to a single column, stacking left-column content (overview and shortlist summary) above right-column content (filter bar, results grid, pagination) while maintaining the same vertical spacing rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchors all page-level sections in normal document flow and manages the two-column grid within a centered max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The acting-tab-switcher is positioned directly below the main 'Acting' heading, centered within the same max-width content band. It sits about 16–24px beneath the title, forming a compact horizontal strip of pill-shaped tabs that spans roughly 60–70% of the content width, leaving comfortable white space on both sides. The tab row itself is approximately 40–56px tall, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding around each tab label, mirroring the airy pill style of the audio players in the mockup. On desktop, tabs are aligned in a single row centered or slightly left-aligned within the content column; on smaller screens, the switcher stretches closer to full width and may become horizontally scrollable while still sitting immediately under the title. Vertically, a 24–32px gap separates the tab row from the start of the two-column section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sits in normal flow between the title and the main two-column content block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The acting-overview-panel occupies the top of the left column in the main two-column area. It begins roughly 32px below the acting-tab-switcher and aligns its top edge horizontally with the showreels-filter-bar in the right column. The panel spans the full width of the left column (about 40% of the content band), with 16–24px internal padding and a tall, portrait-oriented content block reminiscent of the mockup’s rounded hero image. Its outer shape is a rounded rectangle, with pronounced corner radius that visually echoes the pill-style players on the right. The panel’s height is designed to be visually substantial—roughly 45–60% of the initial viewport height—so that it balances the vertical stack of showreel cards on the right when first loaded. Above and below the panel, 24–32px of whitespace separate it from adjacent elements, with the casting-list-summary card sitting directly underneath in the same column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; on taller pages it scrolls normally until potentially scrolled offscreen while the user explores the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The showreels-filter-bar sits at the top of the right column, horizontally aligned with the top of the acting-overview-panel in the left column. It spans the full width of the right column (approx. 60% of the content width) and is relatively shallow—about 56–72px tall—with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside a light, pill-edged or subtly rounded rectangle. Filter controls (e.g., dropdowns, chips, sort toggles) are laid out from left to right, with a small 8–16px gap between controls, keeping the bar compact but not crowded. There is a clear 16–24px vertical gap below the filter bar before the first showreel-inline-player row begins, reinforcing the stacked pattern seen in the audio list from the mockup. On mobile, the filter bar stretches to full width (now a single column) and may wrap its controls into two lines while maintaining the same generous padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the right column; optionally could become sticky within the right column on long result lists, but by default it scrolls with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The showreels-results-grid is directly below the showreels-filter-bar in the right column, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space. To visually mirror the mockup’s vertical list of audio players, the grid defaults to a single-column stack of showreel-inline-player cards that each span nearly the full width of the right column. Each row is separated from the next by 16–24px of whitespace, creating a clear, rhythmical vertical ladder for scanning. The grid itself occupies the majority of the right column’s vertical height, extending down the page as more showreels are listed. On wider viewports where more density is desired, the grid may optionally present as two columns of cards within the right column, but the primary layout intent is a vertical list reminiscent of pill-style players. The left edge of the grid aligns with the left edge of the filter bar above, and the right edge aligns with the right edge of the right column, maintaining a clean columnar structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, expanding vertically as more results are loaded; its size and scroll height define most of the scrolling experience in the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each showreel-inline-player appears as an individual row inside the showreels-results-grid, visually echoing a pill-shaped audio player. It spans almost the full width of the right column, with a small 8–12px inset from the column edges if using card shadows, and features a rounded rectangle container with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. The card height is typically 72–96px, adjustable based on metadata included. Inside the card, a thumbnail or still frame sits on the left, followed by the showreel title and brief metadata in the center, while the primary playback controls cluster towards the left or center-right. The add-to-casting-list-action sits at the far right end of the card, vertically centered, maintaining a clear left-to-right interaction flow: identify, play, then shortlist. The space between consecutive inline players is consistent (16–24px), creating the strong vertical scanning flow analogous to the stacked audio tracks in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results grid; repeats vertically as a list of cards. Hover and focus states can subtly lift or highlight the pill without changing its spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-casting-list-action lives inside each showreel-inline-player card, positioned at the far right of the card’s content area. It appears as a compact, pill-shaped button or icon-plus-label element, vertically centered within the row. Horizontally, it occupies roughly the rightmost 20–25% of the card’s width at most, leaving ample central space for title and playback controls. There is at least 12–16px of padding between the button and the card’s right edge, matching the padding on other sides. The button height aligns with the visual rhythm of the card—roughly 32–40px tall with rounded ends—so it feels like a natural extension of the pill aesthetic rather than a separate block. On narrow screens where horizontal space is constrained, the action shifts to a full-width bar beneath the main card content, with a 8–12px vertical gap separating it from the playback controls, still clearly associated with that showreel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within each inline-player card; it does not float or stick independently but moves with the card in normal scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The casting-list-summary sits in the left column, directly beneath the acting-overview-panel, separated by a 24px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the left column and is styled as a compact card with 16–24px internal padding and a rounded rectangle silhouette to stay consistent with the overall pill/rounded aesthetic. Its height is modest—often 120–200px depending on content—so it does not overpower the hero-like overview panel above, but it remains visually distinct due to a light border, subtle shadow, or muted background tint. In the initial viewport, the top of the summary card may sit roughly aligned with the mid-to-upper region of the showreels list on the right, making it easy to glance between selected items and available reels. As the user scrolls through the lengthy results in the right column, the summary card can adopt a sticky behavior within the left column: once its top edge reaches a comfortable offset from the viewport top (e.g., 80–96px), it remains fixed there while the right column continues to scroll, ensuring the shortlist is always accessible without breaking the two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static until scrolled into position, then sticky within the left column (fixed relative to the viewport with a top offset), maintaining visibility while the user scrolls the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The showreels-pagination-controls are positioned directly below the last row of the showreels-results-grid in the right column. A generous 24–32px vertical gap separates the final showreel-inline-player from the pagination controls, giving a clear sense of completion to the current page of results. The controls themselves occupy a low-profile horizontal strip that aligns with the right edge of the right column; pagination buttons (numbers, next/prev) are right-aligned, while any result-count text can sit to the left within the same row. The strip’s height is modest—around 40–56px—so it doesn’t visually compete with the main content, and there is 32–48px of bottom padding below it before the end of the content area, mirroring the generous footer whitespace seen in minimalist layouts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the results list; it scrolls offscreen when the user navigates upward and reappears when they return to the bottom of the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The acting-deeplink-state-sync is a non-visual controller that attaches logically to the main content region, particularly the acting-tab-switcher, showreels-filter-bar, showreels-results-grid, and showreels-pagination-controls. It occupies no visible space on the page and does not affect layout measurements. Conceptually, it sits at the level of the acting-page-controller, listening for changes in the selected tab, applied filters, current page, and possibly the current casting shortlist, and synchronizes these with deep links or URL parameters. Its presence ensures that when users navigate directly to a filtered or paginated state, the visual layout described above is restored in the same configuration, but its elements remain invisible and non-interactive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-size; it does not participate in visual layout or flow but operates as an invisible overlay or hook bound to the main content container.

name: Showreel Detail
title: Showreel Detail
description: Displays full details for a single acting showreel, allows casting users to review casting-relevant information, add the showreel to a casting list, and navigate between showreels while preserving any list filters via URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display a single showreel using getShowreelDetail(showreelId), including video playback, runtime, year filmed, genre label, description, accent, age range, notable credits, and tags.
Expose casting-focused metadata in a structured block so casting users can quickly evaluate suitability (accent, age range, notable credits, tags).
Provide an 'Add to Casting List' action that calls addShowreelToCastingList(showreelId), reflecting the updated casting list state and preventing duplicate additions.
Retrieve the current casting list using getCastingList() and display a compact summary (e.g., total items) to give context while browsing showreels.

Offer a breadcrumb/link back to the acting showreel list (acting.html) that preserves any originating filters and sorting via URL parameters (return_genre, return_min_year, return_sort_by, return_page).
Provide Next and Previous showreel navigation controls that change the id URL parameter while preserving return_* parameters so users can step through the same filtered subset of showreels.
user_workflows:
View a showreel from a filtered list:
1. User is on acting.html with filters applied (genre, min_year, sort_by, page) and clicks a showreel.
2. acting.html navigates to showreel_detail.html with id and corresponding return_* URL parameters.
3. On load, showreel_detail.html reads id and calls getShowreelDetail(id) to get showreel data.
4. In parallel or after, the page calls getCastingList() to get current casting list context.
5. The showreel video, metadata, and casting list status (is_in_casting_list) are displayed.
6. User reviews the reel, optionally adds it to the casting list, then clicks 'Back to filtered showreels'.
7. The page navigates to acting.html with tab=showreels and the original filter/sort/page parameters restored from return_*.

Add the current showreel to the casting list:
1. User is viewing showreel_detail.html for a given id.
2. The page has already fetched showreel details via getShowreelDetail(id), including is_in_casting_list.
3. If is_in_casting_list indicates the reel is not yet selected, the 'Add to Casting List' button is enabled.
4. User clicks the button; the page calls addShowreelToCastingList(id).
5. On success, the page updates the local casting-list state from the response.casting_list, updates is_in_casting_list for this showreel, and refreshes the casting list summary indicator.
6. The button state changes to reflect that the reel is now in the casting list (and no additional calls to addShowreelToCastingList are made for further clicks).

Review casting list context while browsing:
1. On page load, showreel_detail.html calls getCastingList().
2. If a casting list exists, the total_items and item details are made available to relevant components.
3. A compact summary (such as a counter) indicates how many showreels are currently shortlisted.
4. When addShowreelToCastingList is used, the summary is updated based on the returned casting_list.total_items and items, ensuring consistent information across the page.

Navigate between showreels while keeping filters:
1. User arrives at showreel_detail.html with id and optional return_* parameters populated from acting.html.
2. The page or server-side context provides nextShowreelId and previousShowreelId, representing the adjacent items in the same filtered list.
3. The Next and Previous buttons are bound to these IDs.
4. When the user clicks Next, the page navigates to showreel_detail.html with id set to nextShowreelId and the same return_* values passed through.
5. The new page instance repeats the data loading flow (getShowreelDetail and getCastingList) for the new id.
6. Previous behaves similarly, using previousShowreelId, allowing the user to move backwards through the filtered sequence.
interactions: Page load: On initial load or when id changes in the URL, the page parses URL parameters (id, return_genre, return_min_year, return_sort_by, return_page) and triggers getShowreelDetail(id). If id is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state instead of calling the interface.

Casting list context fetch: On initial load (after or parallel to getShowreelDetail), the page calls getCastingList(). If a casting list exists, its data is stored in page state and passed to the casting list summary and the add-to-casting components.

Video playback: User interacts with the video player (play, pause, scrub). The video element is bound to showreel.video_url and uses display_duration / duration_seconds for runtime display. Playback interactions do not call any interfaces.

Add to Casting List click: When the user clicks the 'Add to Casting List' button and the current showreel is not already in the casting list, the page calls addShowreelToCastingList(currentShowreel.id), temporarily disables the button while the request is in progress, and handles success or failure. On success, the button state and casting list summary are updated.

Casting list summary refresh: After a successful addShowreelToCastingList call, the page updates its local casting list representation from the response and re-renders any components relying on casting_list.total_items or item-level data.

Back to filtered showreels navigation: When the user clicks the 'Back to filtered showreels' breadcrumb, the page constructs a URL for acting.html including tab=showreels and any return_* parameters that were present on the current page. Parameters that were absent on entry are omitted from the outgoing URL.

Next showreel navigation: When the user clicks the Next control and a nextShowreelId is available, the page navigates to showreel_detail.html with id set to nextShowreelId and with all current return_* parameters preserved. The browser performs a full page load, re-triggering getShowreelDetail and getCastingList for the new id.

Previous showreel navigation: When the user clicks the Previous control and a previousShowreelId is available, the page navigates similarly to Next but with id set to previousShowreelId, again preserving return_* parameters. If no previousShowreelId is available, the Previous control is disabled or hidden.

Error and empty-state handling: If getShowreelDetail fails or returns no data for the given id, the page shows an error state and disables casting actions, Next/Previous, and back navigation that depend on the showreel data. If getCastingList returns exists=false, casting list summary components adapt to show an empty state (e.g., zero items) without attempting to display non-existent list details.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary persistent state for this page is carried entirely via URL parameters. The 'id' parameter identifies which Showreel entity is displayed and is required for calling getShowreelDetail(id). Optional 'return_genre', 'return_min_year', 'return_sort_by', and 'return_page' parameters are treated as opaque values that represent the user's previous filter and sort context on acting.html. On page load, these return_* parameters are read and stored in transient page state (not persisted beyond the current instance). They are used exclusively for constructing outbound URLs back to acting.html ('Back to filtered showreels') and for preserving context when navigating to the next or previous showreel. When building the back-link URL to acting.html, the page always includes tab=showreels and appends each of genre, min_year, sort_by, and page only if the corresponding return_* parameter is present and non-empty. When the user clicks Next or Previous, the page changes the 'id' parameter in the destination URL to the provided nextShowreelId or previousShowreelId while copying through the same return_* values; this ensures that subsequent pages can maintain consistent back-navigation behavior. Casting list state is not stored in URL parameters; it is derived from getCastingList() on each load and from the response of addShowreelToCastingList(). The boolean is_in_casting_list field returned from getShowreelDetail(id) is used for initial button state, and may be reconciled against getCastingList() if necessary, but any updates come from the addShowreelToCastingList response rather than local persistence or localStorage.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters, triggering data fetches for the current showreel and the casting list, and distributing the loaded data and loading/error states to child components.
Displays a 'Back to filtered showreels' link that returns the user to acting.html with the original tab and any stored filters (genre, min_year), sort_by, and page restored via URL parameters.
Renders the showreel video and basic playback controls, displaying the runtime and year filmed alongside or near the player, using the data returned from getShowreelDetail.
Displays casting-relevant showreel metadata such as genre label, accent, age range, notable credits, description, and tags so casting users can quickly assess suitability for roles.

Provides the 'Add to Casting List' button for the current showreel. Uses is_in_casting_list and casting list data to determine button state, calls addShowreelToCastingList(showreelId) when clicked, and updates the state to prevent duplicate additions.

Shows a compact summary of the current casting list (for example, the total number of shortlisted showreels) using the data from getCastingList or from the most recent addShowreelToCastingList response.

Renders Next and Previous controls for stepping through showreels within the same filtered context. Uses provided nextShowreelId and previousShowreelId values to construct navigation URLs that change the id parameter while preserving any return_* parameters.
Manages and displays loading and error states for the showreel detail and casting list fetches, enabling or disabling dependent components based on whether required data is available.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 2-column layout likely based on a centered max-width container (approx. 1080–1200px) with a simple percentage-based grid. Overall layout could easily map to a 12-column system (image ~5 columns, audio list ~7 columns).
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the mockup’s simple vertical flow: full-width black site header (global) at the top → a centered content band inside a max-width container. Inside that container, start with a narrow breadcrumb row, then a generous page title area (as part of the metadata), then a two-column block. In the two-column block, the left column is dominated by the showreel video player (replacing the mockup’s portrait image), and the right column is a vertically stacked panel containing casting metadata, the primary casting action button, and secondary navigation/utilities (prev/next). The casting list summary indicator sits as a thin bar near the bottom of the main content, echoing the linear, stacked structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the mockup’s simple vertical flow: full-width black site header (global) at the top → a centered content band inside a max-width container. Inside that container, start with a narrow breadcrumb row, then a generous page title area (as part of the metadata), then a two-column block. In the two-column block, the left column is dominated by the showreel video player (replacing the mockup’s portrait image), and the right column is a vertically stacked panel containing casting metadata, the primary casting action button, and secondary navigation/utilities (prev/next). The casting list summary indicator sits as a thin bar near the bottom of the main content, echoing the linear, stacked structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a centered content width of roughly 1080–1200px, with 15–20% of the viewport as left/right margin. Vertically, let the global header occupy ~12–15% of viewport height. Below it, allocate ~15–20% of the remaining height to the breadcrumb and metadata/title spacing, and the rest to the two-column content. Within the two-column block, the left (video) column occupies about 5/12 of the width; the right (metadata and actions) occupies about 7/12, mirroring the mockup’s image vs. audio list proportions. The casting list summary indicator and prev/next navigation sit beneath the two-column content with 24–32px spacing between them and the main block.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the page moderately spacious. Use 32–48px vertical gaps between major groups: breadcrumb → title/metadata cap → two-column block → summary bar → prev/next navigation. Within each block, use 8–16px vertical padding for items (metadata rows, buttons, nav links). The video player and primary action button are given ample whitespace to read as key interactive elements, while metadata details form tidy, low-density stacks similar to the audio players in the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the original Z-pattern and left-to-right emphasis. The eye moves across the black header, then drops to the breadcrumb and the main showreel title/casting metadata at the top of the right column. The large video player on the left then pulls focus, after which the gaze travels back to the right column to scan metadata and the casting action button from top to bottom. Finally, the user’s attention flows down to the casting list summary bar and the prev/next navigation, which support onward movement through showreels while preserving URL-based filters.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width black header bar, the Showreel Detail page content sits inside a centered white canvas with wide left and right margins, creating a clean, gallery-like feel. A subtle breadcrumb row runs near the top of this canvas, followed by a generous band where the showreel’s title and key casting metadata introduce the piece. The main body is a two-column layout: on the left, a large, rounded-rectangle video player dominates its column much like the theatrical image in the mockup; on the right, a taller column stacks structured casting details and a prominent casting-list action button in a vertical rhythm reminiscent of the original audio-pill list. Beneath this, a slim casting list summary indicator spans the content width, and a horizontally arranged prev/next navigation row anchors the bottom of the view. The overall effect is a balanced, minimal, black-and-white stage where the showreel video and casting actions are clearly prioritized and easy to scan in a single pass.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is non-visual and anchors logically to the top of the Showreel Detail page’s content hierarchy, just inside the main content container beneath the global header. It does not consume visible space but governs when the rest of the layout becomes visible. Conceptually, it sits above the breadcrumb and the two-column layout; when active, it triggers the state handler to display loading or error states instead of the main video and metadata content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered in the normal layout flow; functions as a logical wrapper around all subsequent components, controlling whether the main page structure (breadcrumb, video, metadata, actions, navigation) is displayed or replaced by a loading/error overlay.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned near the top of the main white content area, the breadcrumb nav sits directly beneath the global black header bar with a 32–40px vertical gap, aligned to the same centered max-width container as the rest of the content. It spans the full content width (about 80–85% of the viewport), but its height is minimal, forming a thin horizontal row. The breadcrumb items are left-aligned, running from left to right, with ample horizontal padding (16–24px) inside the row so they feel airy and unobtrusive. There is another 24–32px gap beneath the breadcrumb before the main showreel metadata/title and the two-column structure begin, ensuring the breadcrumb reads as a subtle, secondary trail above the main stage.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow; it scrolls away with the content, not fixed. It always remains within the same central content width as the video and metadata columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video player occupies the left column of the main two-column section, roughly 5/12 of the central content width. It is placed after the breadcrumb and just below the initial metadata/title band, with a 32–40px gap above it. The player is a prominent rectangular frame with softly rounded corners, echoing the portrait image treatment in the mockup. It maintains a 16:9 or similar cinematic aspect ratio and stretches nearly the full width of the left column, with 16–24px internal padding around its container to prevent it from touching the container edges. Vertically, it aligns with the top of the right column’s metadata block, so that the top edges of the video and metadata area line up horizontally. Below the player, there is additional white space (24–32px) before any potential supporting elements (e.g., captions) or before transitioning to lower sections, ensuring that the video remains the primary focal point on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of a responsive two-column layout. On narrower screens, this column stacks above the right column, with the video stretching to nearly 100% of the container width while preserving its aspect ratio.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The casting metadata block forms the top of the right column in the main two-column layout, taking roughly 7/12 of the content width and extending vertically alongside the video player. It begins at the same vertical height as the top of the video player, with a 32–40px gap below the breadcrumb. Inside this right column, the showreel title sits near the top, set with generous top and bottom padding (16–24px each) to mirror the mockup’s spacious ‘Acting’ heading area. Below the title, metadata items (role, age range, accent, duration, tags, etc.) are arranged in a vertical stack with consistent 8–12px spacing, echoing the stacked audio players but in a more compact form. Each row aligns flush left and does not span the entire page, remaining within the right column to maintain the two-column balance. The block as a whole has 16–24px padding on all sides, visually reading as a clean, lightly framed panel that balances the weight of the large video on the left. There is a 16–24px vertical gap below this metadata stack before any primary action button or other controls in the same column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it grows vertically as metadata entries increase, pushing content below it down while keeping the top aligned to the video player for as long as possible. On smaller screens, it appears directly beneath the video player, maintaining its internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The casting list action button is positioned within the right column, directly associated with the showreel metadata and visually close to the showreel title. It sits immediately below the primary metadata block, separated by a 16–24px vertical gap so it reads as the next logical step after reviewing key details. Horizontally, it is left-aligned with the metadata text, occupying roughly 40–60% of the right column’s width so it appears substantial but not edge-to-edge. The button has generous padding (12–16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal) to match the airy, pill-like controls seen in the mockup’s audio players. There is another 16–24px gap below the button before any additional secondary actions or before the bottom of the right column, ensuring it stands out as the primary call-to-action in that column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, following the metadata vertically. On smaller screens, it appears prominently under the metadata, still left-aligned, and remains easily tappable with comfortable surrounding whitespace.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The casting list summary indicator is placed beneath the main two-column section, spanning the full width of the central content container, like a subtle status bar that anchors the state of the casting basket. There is a 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom of the two-column block (video and metadata/actions) and this summary bar, marking it as a separate but related section. Visually, it forms a low-height horizontal strip (roughly 48–64px tall) with content left-aligned inside (e.g., ‘X items in your casting list’), and some right-aligned utility information or a compact link if needed. It stays within the white background of the main page but can use a light gray fill or a thin top border to subtly differentiate it. The bar uses 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding to keep text readable but not dense. This indicator visually mirrors the linear stacking of elements in the mockup, acting as a bridge between the detailed content above and the navigation below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally sits in the document flow beneath the two-column section. Optionally, on larger screens it can become sticky to the bottom of the viewport when the user scrolls past it, maintaining a slim presence that always reflects the current casting list state while not obscuring the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prev/next navigation row is located near the bottom of the page’s main content area, directly beneath the casting list summary indicator with a 24–32px gap separating them. It spans the same max-width container as the other components and is horizontally oriented, providing directional navigation to the previous and next showreels. Typically, a ‘Previous’ control aligns to the left and a ‘Next’ control aligns to the right, creating a clear left-to-right flow across the width of the page that echoes the header’s navigation pattern. The row is low in height (about 48px), with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding for each control, giving them a pill-like appearance consistent with the site’s minimalist, modern style. There is comfortable whitespace (32–48px) below this nav before the start of any footer content, so it feels like the natural end of the showreel detail section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal content flow at the bottom of the Showreel Detail section. As the user scrolls, it moves off-screen like standard content; it does not stick or float. On narrower screens, the previous and next controls can stack or center-align while retaining vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error and loading state handler overlays or replaces the main content area governed by the showreel-detail-init component. When in a loading state, a centered spinner or minimal loading indicator appears in the middle of the main content band (between the header and where the two-column layout would be), with generous empty space around it, preserving the minimalist feel and clearly signposting that content is being fetched. When an error occurs, a compact message box appears similarly centered within the content width, with 24–32px padding and possibly a subtle border or tinted background, but still respecting the same left/right margins as the video and metadata. In both cases, the underlying components (breadcrumb, video, metadata, actions, summary bar, prev/next) are hidden or visually subdued, so the user’s attention is fully captured by the state message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay or conditional replacement. It sits at the same vertical level as the main content but, when active, visually supersedes all other components in the main container. It centers its content both horizontally and in the upper-middle of the viewport for quick recognition, and scrolls naturally when its content exceeds the viewport height.

name: Rates & Quotes
title: Rates & Quotes
description: Page to review baseline rate information, configure an instant quote, and submit a project-specific quote inquiry.

core_features: Load and display baseline rate information, example packages, and FAQs using getRatesAndQuotesPageContent.
Load calculator configuration (project types, usage durations, territories, and default values) using getRateCalculatorOptions.
Initialize the Instant Quote Calculator using URL parameters when present, otherwise using default_values from getRateCalculatorOptions.
Allow users to adjust calculator inputs (project type, script length/duration, usage duration, territory) and dynamically request an estimated fee using calculateInstantRateQuote.
Display the estimated fee returned by calculateInstantRateQuote and compare it against an optional target budget provided by URL parameter target_budget_usd.
Provide a control to copy the current calculator configuration and estimate into a quote inquiry form and reveal that form on the same page.
Allow users to fill out and submit the quote inquiry form with contact details and project notes, sending all data to submitQuoteInquiry.
Provide navigation from the inquiry area to contact_booking.html with the booking tab selected, for users who prefer direct booking instead of a quote.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View baseline rates and FAQs
1. User opens rates.html via navigation or direct link.
2. Page calls getRatesAndQuotesPageContent.
3. The overview, rate_guides, and FAQs content are rendered in dedicated sections.
4. User reviews baseline rate guidance and FAQs to understand typical pricing and what affects quotes.

Workflow: Generate an instant quote from scratch
1. User opens rates.html without URL parameters.
2. Page calls getRateCalculatorOptions and gets default_values.
3. Instant Quote Calculator initializes its state from default_values (project_type_id, script_length_seconds, usage_duration_id, territory_id, target_budget_usd if provided there).
4. Calculator automatically calls calculateInstantRateQuote with the initial configuration and displays the returned estimated_fee.
5. User adjusts calculator inputs (project type, script length, usage duration, territory).
6. On each change, the calculator debounces and calls calculateInstantRateQuote with the updated configuration.
7. The new estimated_fee replaces the previous one and any budget comparison is updated accordingly.

Workflow: Generate an instant quote from a preconfigured link
1. User clicks a link from another page or an external email that includes URL parameters (e.g., project_type, script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory, target_budget_usd).
2. When rates.html loads, it calls getRateCalculatorOptions.
3. The page derives initial calculator state by combining URL parameters with the available options from getRateCalculatorOptions (only applying URL parameter values that match returned option ids).
4. Calculator calls calculateInstantRateQuote using the derived state.
5. Estimated fee and budget comparison (using target_budget_usd URL parameter if present) are displayed so the user can immediately see whether the configuration aligns with their budget.
6. User can refine inputs further, triggering new calculateInstantRateQuote calls as they adjust fields.

Workflow: Copy quote details into inquiry form and submit
1. After obtaining a satisfactory estimated fee in the calculator, the user clicks the 'Copy this quote to inquiry form' control.
2. The page reveals the quote inquiry form section if it was collapsed or not yet visible.
3. The form pre-populates non-contact fields (project_type, script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory, estimated_fee, budget_limit) from the current calculator state and any active budget indicator.
4. User enters name, email, and a free-text project message, optionally editing any prefilled numeric budget or configuration fields as needed.
5. User submits the form, triggering submitQuoteInquiry with all required parameters and the current calculator configuration.
6. On successful response (success = true), the form shows a confirmation state using the returned message and may disable re-submission or clear inputs.
7. If success = false, the form displays the returned message as an error and allows corrections and resubmission.

Workflow: Switch from requesting a quote to booking directly
1. While viewing the inquiry form section, user sees a text link offering direct booking instead of a quote.
2. User clicks the link.
3. Browser navigates to contact_booking.html with URL parameter tab=booking.
4. Any quote configuration remains on the Rates & Quotes page URL for later reuse if the user navigates back.

Workflow: Share a configured quote view via URL
1. User adjusts calculator inputs to represent a desired configuration.
2. On each significant change (e.g., project type, script length, usage duration, territory, target budget input), the page updates the browser URL query parameters using navigation APIs (e.g., pushState/replaceState) to reflect the current calculator state without page reload.
3. User copies the URL from the address bar and shares it with colleagues.
4. When the recipient opens the URL, the preconfigured workflow runs: the calculator initializes from URL parameters and displays the corresponding estimate after calling calculateInstantRateQuote.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load (no URL parameters)
- Triggers: Window onLoad.
- Actions: Call getRatesAndQuotesPageContent and getRateCalculatorOptions in parallel. Initialize calculator state from getRateCalculatorOptions.default_values. Call calculateInstantRateQuote with initial configuration. Render baseline content, calculator controls, and empty inquiry form (collapsed or not pre-filled).

Interaction: Page load (with URL parameters)
- Triggers: Window onLoad when URL includes project_type, script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory, and/or target_budget_usd.
- Actions: Call getRatesAndQuotesPageContent and getRateCalculatorOptions. Merge URL parameters with options from getRateCalculatorOptions (only use ids that match available options). Use merged values as initial calculator state; any missing values fall back to default_values. Call calculateInstantRateQuote with these values. Use target_budget_usd from URL (if present) to initialize budget comparison display and the future budget_limit field in the inquiry form.

Interaction: Change project type in calculator
- Triggers: onChange event on project type selection input.
- Actions: Update calculator state for project_type. Optionally update URL parameter project_type. Call calculateInstantRateQuote with updated state (project_type, current script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory). Update estimated fee and budget comparison display.

Interaction: Change script length/duration in calculator
- Triggers: onChange/onInput event on script length field.
- Actions: Validate input as a positive number. Update calculator state for script_length_seconds. Optionally update URL parameter script_length_seconds. Debounced call to calculateInstantRateQuote. Update estimated fee and budget comparison display.

Interaction: Change usage duration in calculator
- Triggers: onChange event on usage duration selection.
- Actions: Update calculator state for usage_duration. Optionally update URL parameter usage_duration. Call calculateInstantRateQuote with updated configuration. Update estimated fee and budget comparison display.

Interaction: Change territory in calculator
- Triggers: onChange event on territory selection.
- Actions: Update calculator state for territory. Optionally update URL parameter territory. Call calculateInstantRateQuote with updated configuration. Update estimated fee and budget comparison display.

Interaction: Adjust target budget within calculator (if editable field is provided)
- Triggers: onChange/onInput event on budget input control.
- Actions: Update internal target budget state. Update URL parameter target_budget_usd. Recalculate budget comparison indicator (e.g., whether estimated_fee is above, equal to, or below target_budget_usd) without calling calculateInstantRateQuote, since the estimate itself does not change. Ensure this value is ready to pass as budget_limit to submitQuoteInquiry.

Interaction: Click 'Copy this quote to inquiry form' button
- Triggers: onClick event on the copy button.
- Actions: If the inquiry form section is collapsed or hidden, expand/reveal it. Pre-fill form fields for project_type, script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory, estimated_fee, and budget_limit from the current calculator state and most recent calculateInstantRateQuote response. Scroll to the inquiry form section for user convenience.

Interaction: Edit prefilled configuration inside inquiry form
- Triggers: onChange/onInput/onSelect events on configuration-related fields within the inquiry form (if these are editable instead of read-only).
- Actions: Update local form state and ensure that the values used in submitQuoteInquiry reflect the edited configuration. This does not automatically update the calculator view or call calculateInstantRateQuote unless explicitly configured to do so.

Interaction: Submit quote inquiry form
- Triggers: onSubmit event on the inquiry form.
- Actions: Validate required fields (name, email, message, project_type, script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory). If validation passes, call submitQuoteInquiry with name, email, message, project_type, script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory, latest estimated_fee from calculator state (if available), and budget_limit (from either target_budget_usd or user input). Handle the response: if success is true, display the success message and optionally disable or reset the form; if false, display the error message and allow corrections.

Interaction: Click 'Prefer to book a session directly?' link
- Triggers: onClick event on a navigation link near the inquiry form.
- Actions: Navigate to contact_booking.html with URL parameter tab=booking. No backend interface is invoked from this page; navigation is handled by the browser.

Interaction: FAQ expand/collapse (if implemented as accordions)
- Triggers: onClick event on FAQ question headers.
- Actions: Toggle the visibility of the associated FAQ answer text from faqs_html. No backend call is required, as all FAQ content is already provided by getRatesAndQuotesPageContent.

Interaction: Refresh or revisit the page
- Triggers: Browser reload or navigation back to rates.html.
- Actions: Re-run the initial page load logic, reapplying any URL parameters to restore calculator state and the visible estimate, ensuring consistent behavior across sessions without using localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by a combination of API responses and URL parameters, without any reliance on localStorage or other client-side persistence. On initial load, the page calls getRatesAndQuotesPageContent and getRateCalculatorOptions. The calculator state fields (project_type, script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory, and optional target_budget_usd) are derived as follows: (1) For each field, if a corresponding URL parameter is present and matches a valid option id returned by getRateCalculatorOptions (for project_type, usage_duration, territory) or is a valid number (for script_length_seconds and target_budget_usd), that value is used; (2) If a field is not provided or invalid in the URL, the value from getRateCalculatorOptions.default_values is used; (3) If neither a URL parameter nor a valid default is available, the UI should prompt the user to make a selection before enabling calculateInstantRateQuote. After determining initial state, the page calls calculateInstantRateQuote once and stores the returned rate_quote (particularly estimated_fee) in memory for display. Whenever the user changes calculator inputs, the state is updated and calculateInstantRateQuote is called again with the new configuration; the returned estimate replaces the previous one. In parallel, the page may update the browser URL using navigation APIs to encode the current calculator state into query parameters, allowing the configuration to be shareable and restorable on reload. The optional target_budget_usd URL parameter and any user-entered budget input are mirrored in an internal budget state and used only for display (budget comparison) and as the budget_limit value for submitQuoteInquiry. When the user clicks 'Copy this quote to inquiry form', the current calculator state and last known estimated_fee are copied into the inquiry form's state; these values can be edited in the form without affecting the calculator unless explicitly synchronized. On inquiry submission, submitQuoteInquiry is called using the inquiry form state. No additional entities are loaded via interfaces beyond those exposed by the four assigned methods, and all persistent records related to quotes and inquiries are created via calculateInstantRateQuote (RateQuote) and submitQuoteInquiry (QuoteInquiry).

components.functionality: Fetches and displays the general rates overview and explanatory text for the page, giving context on how rates are determined and what factors influence the quote calculator.
Displays a structured list or table of baseline rate guides and example packages for different service types, helping users understand typical starting points before using the calculator.
Shows frequently asked questions and answers related to rates, usage, and quoting. May allow expand/collapse interactions for individual FAQs.

Central component that hosts all calculator inputs (project type, script length/duration, usage duration, territory, optional target budget), initializes them from URL parameters and defaults, and coordinates calls to calculateInstantRateQuote whenever inputs change.

Displays the current estimated fee, the configuration summary (project type, script length, usage duration, territory), and a visual or textual indicator of how the estimate relates to any target budget.
Provides a single action for users to transfer the current calculator configuration and estimated fee into the quote inquiry form, and to reveal or focus that form section.

Captures user contact details (name, email) and project message along with configuration fields (project_type, script_length_seconds, usage_duration, territory, estimated_fee, budget_limit) and submits them to create a QuoteInquiry via submitQuoteInquiry.
Offers an alternative path for users who prefer to book a session directly instead of, or in addition to, requesting a quote, by navigating to the booking tab on the contact/booking page.
