It sits in the same horizontal line as the section title and favorites-counter but is right-justified, with about 8–16px spacing between the control and any adjacent elements. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space between this header row and the start of the favorite-clients-list beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within the favorites-section header; scrolls with the section and retains its relative position as content height changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-clients-list sits directly under the favorites-section header row, with 16–24px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the section’s inner area and uses a vertical stacking pattern similar to the main testimonial-list, but with a slightly lighter or more compact card style to denote their curated nature. Each favorite client entry occupies a single row, with 12–20px vertical spacing between rows. Items are narrower in height than full testimonial cards, typically around 56–80px, and contain brief information (client name, logo or initial, perhaps a small tag) plus a remove-from-favorites icon aligned to the right. The list’s overall height is typically shorter; if many favorites exist, the list expands and pushes the bottom of the section downward naturally.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertical list within its parent section; it grows and shrinks with the number of favorite items and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-control is positioned at the bottom of the testimonials-section, directly beneath the testimonial-list, with 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the last testimonial-item. It occupies a horizontal strip about 40–56px tall and spans the width of the section’s inner area. The pagination elements (previous/next buttons, page numbers) are right-aligned within this strip to balance the left-heavy content of the testimonial cards, echoing the subtle asymmetry seen in the mockup’s audio controls. There is some internal spacing (8–16px) between page buttons to keep the control airy. The control visually anchors the testimonial list by forming a clean horizontal line at the bottom of the section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer control of the testimonials-section; it remains at the bottom of the section’s content and scrolls off-screen naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-counter appears within the header row of the favorites-section, adjacent to the section title. It is typically a small pill-shaped badge containing a number (e.g., ‘5’) and possibly a heart icon, about 24–28px tall and 32–48px wide depending on content. It sits inline with the title on the left side of the header row, separated from the title text by 8–12px of horizontal space. The badge uses a slightly higher-contrast background (e.g., dark fill with light text) to stand out against the white card, subtly drawing attention to the number of favorites without overshadowing the main testimonials. Its small size maintains the minimalist feel while providing a clear status indicator.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline status indicator anchored in the favorites-section header; it moves only as that section moves and does not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-link is positioned in the left column below the client-type-legend, serving as a strong yet simple call-to-action. There is a generous 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom of the legend card and the contact-link, which helps it stand out. The contact-link is styled as a pill-shaped button or bold text link, aligned to the left column’s baseline but not spanning the full width—typically about 160–220px wide and 40–48px tall if rendered as a button. It sits within the left column’s flow such that, on most screens, it is roughly aligned vertically with the mid-to-lower portion of the testimonials-section on the right, encouraging users who have scanned testimonials to return left and consider making contact. On very narrow screens where columns stack, the contact-link appears below the legend but before the right-column content, maintaining clear visibility.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation/CTA element anchored in the left column; scrolls normally with the page and does not fix to the viewport.

name: Project Planner
title: Project Planner
description: Configure a combined voiceover and acting project by selecting services, tracking total session duration against a recommended maximum, adding budget and priority notes, and saving the project plan for follow-up actions.

core_features: Load planner configuration (intro text, tips, and recommended maximum duration) using getProjectPlannerPageContent.
Load available voiceover, acting, and other service options using getProjectPlannerServiceOptions and present them as selectable line items with adjustable quantities.
Maintain an in-page project plan state composed of selected service options and quantities, and continuously compute total session duration from the loaded service_option session_duration_hours.

Display a duration summary indicator that compares the computed total duration to the max_recommended_duration_hours from getProjectPlannerPageContent, highlighting whether the plan is within the recommended range.
Allow users to start or reset a plan via a 'New Project' control, optionally prefilled with a title from the URL parameter.
Provide fields for budget notes and additional instructions, optionally annotated with a target budget hint from the target_budget_usd URL parameter.
Save the current project configuration (title, budget note, notes, and service_items) using saveProjectPlan and show the returned confirmation details.
From the configured plan, navigate to rates.html with a derived target_budget_usd parameter to estimate rates for the plan.
From the configured plan, navigate to contact_booking.html with tab=booking to request a booking based on the plan.
user_workflows:
Create a new plan from scratch:
1. User opens project_planner.html directly or via navigation.
2. The page controller reads URL parameters (title, target_budget_usd) and invokes getProjectPlannerPageContent and getProjectPlannerServiceOptions in parallel.
3. Once loaded, the title input is initialized (using the title URL parameter if present), and the budget note area displays or hints at the target_budget_usd value if present.
4. The user clicks 'New Project' if they want to explicitly initialize a fresh plan; the page clears any existing plan state and reapplies the default title (from URL if available).
5. The user selects one or more services in the Voiceover, Acting, and Other sections and adjusts quantities as needed.
6. As the user adjusts selections, the duration summary indicator updates the total duration and whether it is within the recommended maximum.
7. The user enters budget notes and additional instructions.
8. The user clicks 'Save Project Plan'; the page sends title, budget_note, notes, and the current service_items array to saveProjectPlan.
9. On success, the page shows confirmation using the returned project_plan and service_items; if it fails, an error message is displayed.

Start a plan prefilled from another page:
1. User clicks a link to project_planner.html from index.html, voiceover.html, or acting.html with a title URL parameter set.
2. On load, the controller reads the title parameter and uses it as the default title in the Project Title field.
3. The rest of the workflow follows the same steps as creating a new plan from scratch (select services, adjust quantities, add notes, save).

Use a reference budget from the rates page:
1. User navigates from rates.html to project_planner.html with target_budget_usd set in the URL.
2. On load, the controller reads target_budget_usd and displays it next to or within the budget notes section as a numeric reference (without assuming any specific value).
3. The user configures services and notes, possibly copying or adapting the reference budget into a more descriptive budget_note.
4. When the user clicks 'Estimate rates for this plan', the page derives a numeric budget value (derived_budget) based on available information: it first attempts to parse a numeric budget from the budget_note field; if no parseable numeric value is present, it falls back to the incoming target_budget_usd if it exists.
5. The page navigates to rates.html, adding target_budget_usd=derived_budget to the URL query string when a numeric derived_budget is available.
6. The user can then adjust rates settings on rates.html with this budget as context.

Stay within recommended session duration:
1. After services are loaded, the duration summary shows an initial total duration based on the current plan state (typically zero if nothing is selected).
2. As the user includes services and increases quantities, the page computes the total_duration_hours as the sum of (quantity × session_duration_hours) for each selected service.
3. The duration summary compares total_duration_hours to max_recommended_duration_hours from getProjectPlannerPageContent and updates an indicator (for example, flags when total exceeds the recommended maximum).
4. The user adjusts quantities or removes services until the indicator shows that the plan is within the recommended duration if that is their goal.
5. When the user saves, the saveProjectPlan response includes within_recommended_duration, which is displayed for confirmation.

Request booking based on the plan:
1. The user configures and (optionally) saves a project plan as described above.
2. When satisfied, the user clicks 'Request booking based on this plan'.
3. The page can ensure that there is a current in-memory plan state (preferably saved, but not strictly required for navigation) and then navigates to contact_booking.html with tab=booking in the query string.
4. The booking page opens on its booking tab, where the user can provide detailed scheduling information, referencing their plan manually if needed.
interactions: On page load, the controller parses URL query parameters (title, target_budget_usd) and invokes getProjectPlannerPageContent and getProjectPlannerServiceOptions; it then initializes the page state when both responses are available.

User clicks the 'New Project' button: the current in-memory project plan is reset (title reset to default from URL if present, all quantities cleared, notes cleared), without re-calling the APIs unless needed.
User edits the project title text field: the in-memory plan title is updated in real-time; any subsequent saveProjectPlan call uses this updated title.

User includes or excludes a specific service: they either check/uncheck a selection control or change the quantity from zero to a positive number; the page updates the corresponding service item in the in-memory plan and recalculates the total duration.

User changes a service quantity: the quantity input for that service is updated, the corresponding service item's total_duration_hours is recalculated as quantity × session_duration_hours, and the overall total_duration_hours is updated.

The duration summary indicator listens to changes in the in-memory service_items list: whenever the list or any quantity changes, it recomputes the total duration and updates the status (e.g., within or beyond recommended duration).

User focuses the budget notes field: if a target_budget_usd URL parameter is available and not yet reflected in the budget_note, the UI can show contextual helper text referencing that value; it does not overwrite user input.

User types in the budget notes field: the in-memory budget_note is updated; later, when deriving a numeric budget for navigation to rates.html, the page attempts to parse any numeric value from this text.
User types in the additional instructions field: the in-memory notes property is updated.

User clicks 'Save Project Plan': the page validates that a non-empty title exists, constructs a service_items array from the selected services (including service_option_id, quantity, and session_duration_hours derived from the corresponding ServiceOption entries), and calls saveProjectPlan.

On successful saveProjectPlan response: the page updates the in-memory plan state with values from project_plan (including total_duration_hours and within_recommended_duration), displays a success message using message, and may render a summary of the saved service_items as returned by the API.
On saveProjectPlan failure (success=false): the page displays the provided message in an error state and leaves the current in-memory plan unchanged so the user can correct inputs.

User clicks 'Estimate rates for this plan': the page calculates derived_budget (prioritizing numeric parsing from budget_note, falling back to incoming target_budget_usd if available), builds a URL for rates.html, optionally including target_budget_usd in the query when a valid numeric value exists, and navigates the browser to that URL.
User clicks 'Request booking based on this plan': the page builds a URL for contact_booking.html with tab=booking in the query string and navigates the browser to that URL.

If the URL changes while the user is on the planner (for example, via navigation that updates query parameters without a full reload), the controller can re-read the parameters and, if appropriate, update default title or budget hints without overwriting current user-entered values unless explicitly reset.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is held in memory while the user interacts with the planner and is initialized using URL parameters and API responses. URL parameters influence only initial or contextual state and are not used to persist full project plan data.

- URL Parameter: title
  - If present when the page loads, this value is used as the initial default for the project title field.
  - When the user clicks 'New Project', if they have not entered a custom title since load, the field can be reset to this default. If the user has edited the title, the reset can either revert to this parameter-based default or to an empty value, according to the implementation decision; in all cases, the parameter itself is not changed by user actions.
  - The title parameter is not updated by this page and is not used after initial defaulting; subsequent title changes are held only in the in-memory state.

- URL Parameter: target_budget_usd
  - If present when the page loads, this numeric value is exposed as a reference for the user in the budget area (e.g., helper text or placeholder), but it does not overwrite any user-entered budget_note content.
  - When the user triggers navigation to rates.html via 'Estimate rates for this plan', the page computes derived_budget. This value is determined by first attempting to parse a numeric amount from the current budget_note field; if no numeric value can be extracted, the logic uses the incoming target_budget_usd if it exists. If neither yields a valid numeric amount, the page navigates to rates.html without adding the target_budget_usd query parameter.
  - The planner does not update or persist target_budget_usd itself; the value is only consumed for context or forwarded as part of navigation.

- API-driven state:
  - getProjectPlannerPageContent provides intro_html, tips_html, and max_recommended_duration_hours. These values are stored in an in-memory configuration state. max_recommended_duration_hours is used whenever the duration summary needs to determine whether the current total is within the recommended maximum.
  - getProjectPlannerServiceOptions returns arrays of active and inactive services for voiceover, acting, and other categories. These arrays form the basis of selectable services. Inactive services (is_active=false) are either not displayed or displayed as disabled, but are never included in the plan.
  - The in-memory plan's service_items state is derived by matching selected services to these arrays. For each selected service_option_id, the component stores the quantity and the associated session_duration_hours from the loaded ServiceOption entry.

- Computed state (total duration and indicator):
  - total_duration_hours is computed client-side as the sum of total_duration_hours for each in-memory service item. Each item's total_duration_hours is computed from its quantity and session_duration_hours. These computed values are used both to populate the payload for saveProjectPlan and to display the duration summary.
  - The duration summary uses max_recommended_duration_hours from getProjectPlannerPageContent to determine whether the current total is within or above the recommended limit. This logic is purely client-side; when saveProjectPlan is called, the back-end computes and returns its own total_duration_hours and within_recommended_duration, which are then displayed as authoritative confirmation.

- Saving state:
  - When the user clicks 'Save Project Plan', the current in-memory plan (title, budget_note, notes, and service_items) is sent to saveProjectPlan. The response includes a project_plan object and a normalized service_items list. The page then updates its in-memory plan representation with these returned values.
  - The ProjectPlan.id returned by saveProjectPlan is not propagated into the URL (because no such URL parameter is defined for this page). Thus, reloading the page or navigating away and back will not automatically restore this saved plan; instead, the planner always starts from a new, unsaved state derived from URL parameters and API defaults.

- Navigation state:
  - When navigating to rates.html, the planner constructs a URL that may include target_budget_usd based on derived_budget. This influences only the rates page; upon returning to the planner (if a link back exists), that page may supply its own title or target_budget_usd parameters again.
  - When navigating to contact_booking.html, the planner always includes tab=booking in the query to ensure that the booking interface is preselected. No project-specific identifiers are passed via URL from this page, so any plan reference must be re-entered or handled by the booking page using its own logic.

No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; state is reset whenever the page is reloaded, and only URL parameters and API responses affect the initial configuration.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization by reading URL parameters, calling getProjectPlannerPageContent and getProjectPlannerServiceOptions, and seeding the in-memory project plan state with defaults.
Displays the intro and tips content for the planner using HTML fragments returned by getProjectPlannerPageContent; does not store its own state beyond rendering.

Provides the 'New Project' button and the editable project title field. Initializes the title from the URL parameter if available and updates the in-memory project plan title as the user types or resets the plan when 'New Project' is clicked.

Lists all voiceover services from getProjectPlannerServiceOptions.voiceover_services that are active. For each service, allows the user to include/exclude it and adjust quantity; updates the in-memory ProjectPlanServiceItem list and triggers total duration recalculation.

Lists all acting services from getProjectPlannerServiceOptions.acting_services that are active. For each service, allows inclusion/exclusion and quantity adjustments, updating the in-memory plan and triggering duration recalculation.

Lists all other services from getProjectPlannerServiceOptions.other_services that are active, with the same selection and quantity controls as the voiceover and acting selectors, contributing to the overall plan.

Displays the current total session duration, derived from all selected services and quantities, and compares it to max_recommended_duration_hours from getProjectPlannerPageContent, indicating whether the plan is within the recommended duration.

Provides inputs for budget notes and additional instructions. Uses target_budget_usd from the URL (if present) as a contextual reference for the budget field and keeps the in-memory project Plan budget_note and notes in sync with user input.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 2-column layout likely based on a centered max-width container (approx. 1080–1200px) with a simple percentage-based grid. Overall layout could easily map to a 12-column system (image ~5 columns, audio list ~7 columns).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow inside an existing full-width, fixed black header. Below the header, the main content is centered in a max-width container (around 1080–1200px) with a generous vertical gap before the first element. The first band is the project-title-bar (with the page title and right-aligned init controller), followed by the planner-intro-and-tips block as a full-width but text-focused section. Beneath this, the layout shifts into the familiar two-column structure: the left column houses the duration-summary-indicator at the top and the budget-and-notes-section beneath it, while the right column stacks the three service-selector lists (voiceover, then acting, then other) full-width within that column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow inside an existing full-width, fixed black header. Below the header, the main content is centered in a max-width container (around 1080–1200px) with a generous vertical gap before the first element. The first band is the project-title-bar (with the page title and right-aligned init controller), followed by the planner-intro-and-tips block as a full-width but text-focused section. Beneath this, the layout shifts into the familiar two-column structure: the left column houses the duration-summary-indicator at the top and the budget-and-notes-section beneath it, while the right column stacks the three service-selector lists (voiceover, then acting, then other) full-width within that column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed header keeps to roughly 12–15% of viewport height. The top title + intro area consumes about 20–25% of the remaining viewport height, with ample vertical spacing (32–48px) above and below to echo the dramatic breathing room of the 'Acting' heading in the mockup. The main two-column project planning area takes up the rest of the vertical space, growing scrollable as content expands. Horizontally, the central content band spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport, leaving about 15–20% of viewport width as combined left/right margins. Within that band, the two columns split roughly 40% (left) to 60% (right), mirroring the hero-image vs. audio-list balance from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally moderately spacious. Major sections (title bar, intro/tips, two-column grid) are separated by 32–64px of vertical space. Components within a section use 16–24px vertical spacing between them, and 16–24px internal padding, matching the airy, pill-like feel seen in the audio players. No dense forms or tables appear; form sections and selectors are composed as discrete cards with clear boundaries and whitespace, so each major decision area (selecting services, reviewing duration, adding notes) feels focused and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the mockup’s Z-pattern and vertical scanning behavior. The eye first sweeps left-to-right across the fixed black header, then drops to the bold 'Project Planner' title in the project-title-bar. From there, attention moves slightly right to the project-planner-init-controller, then down to the planner-intro-and-tips block. As the user scrolls into the main planning area, the eye is drawn first to the left column’s duration-summary-indicator (analogous to the large image in the mockup), then moves horizontally to the right column to explore the stacked service selectors from top to bottom, mirroring the audio-player list’s vertical scanning pattern. Budget-and-notes-section sits below the summary, accessible but understated, picked up naturally after the main service-selection tasks.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed, full-width black header, the Project Planner page opens into a calm, centered white canvas. A generous vertical gap leads into a wide title band where 'Project Planner' anchors the left side in confident, theatrical type, while a compact cluster of planner controls sits to the right, both framed by ample white space top and bottom. Just beneath, a lightly framed intro and tips block stretches across the main content width, offering guidance without feeling dense. As you scroll a bit, the layout resolves into a clear two-column planning grid: on the left, a tall, card-like duration summary panel sits near the top of the viewport and stays visible as you move, acting as the visual anchor in place of the original hero image; beneath it, a more relaxed budget and notes panel invites future-facing comments. On the right, occupying a slightly wider column, three pill-framed service selector sections stack vertically—voiceover first, then acting, then other services—each with generous internal padding and consistent gaps, echoing the vertical rhythm of the audio players from the mockup. The entire composition feels theatrical yet restrained, with a strong central band of content, generous margins, and an easy top-to-bottom reading flow that keeps the planning task approachable and focused.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-planner-init-controller is positioned in the same horizontal band as the project-title-bar, aligned to the far right edge of the centered content container. It sits horizontally level with, or slightly above, the main 'Project Planner' text, creating a subtle right-side counterweight while preserving the title’s dominance. The controller appears as a compact pill-style cluster (for actions like 'New Plan', 'Load Template', or reset) with 8–12px of internal padding and at least 16px of whitespace around it so it does not crowd the title. It occupies roughly 15–20% of the content width on desktop and never more than needed to express its controls. A vertical gap of 24–32px separates this entire title/control band from the planner-intro-and-tips below. On narrower screens, the controller wraps below the main title but remains right-aligned within the content, maintaining a light, unobtrusive presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The controller is part of the normal document flow within the top title band, right-aligned via layout (e.g., flex space-between). It scrolls with the page and does not remain sticky; on small viewports, it stacks beneath the title bar content while preserving right alignment and consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner-intro-and-tips block sits directly below the title/controls band, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px to maintain a clean transition. It spans most of the content width but is visually constrained so it remains easy to read: on large screens, the text line length is limited to about 60–70 characters, effectively occupying around 70–80% of the central container width and aligned with the left edge of the title text. There is generous vertical padding inside the block (16–24px top and bottom) and a light separation from the background (e.g., subtle gray text, maybe a light divider beneath) echoing the understated feel of the mockup’s heading area. A 32–48px gap below this block clearly signals the end of introductory content before the user encounters the denser two-column planning area. On mobile, this block remains full-width and precedes the two-column content, preserving its role as an orienting narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This block behaves as a standard content section within the vertical flow, expanding to full width of the central container and shrinking responsively on smaller screens. It does not stick or float, but it maintains consistent vertical spacing relative to the title band above and the two-column grid below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-title-bar forms the first major content band beneath the global header, establishing the page’s focal point. It is centered within the main content container (max-width ~1080–1200px), with wide left and right margins relative to the viewport. Vertically, it has generous padding—approximately 24–32px above and below the title text—creating a strong but uncluttered presence similar to the 'Acting' heading area. The main title, 'Project Planner', aligns to the left of this band, starting at the same left edge that will later align with the left column in the two-column section. The project-planner-init-controller sits within this same band, aligned to the far right, giving the bar a balanced horizontal structure that subtly echoes the header’s logo → nav → icons flow. A vertical gap of around 8–12px can separate any subtitle or small descriptor from the main title, while the overall title band occupies roughly 20% of the initial viewport height when combined with spacing above and below, making it visually prominent but not overwhelming. Below the bar, there is a clear 24–32px gap leading into the planner-intro-and-tips block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The title bar remains part of the normal scrollable content and sits immediately below the fixed global header. It does not stick as the user scrolls; instead, it anchors the top of the planning experience before the user moves into the two-column planner grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The voiceover-service-selector is the first and topmost section in the right column of the main two-column grid. This grid starts below the planner-intro-and-tips, after a vertical gap of about 40–48px, and is centered within the page; the right column occupies roughly 60% of the content width, starting at the same vertical level as the duration-summary-indicator in the left column. The voiceover selector stretches the full width of the right column, presented as a card-like block with pill-style option rows and clear internal padding (16–24px on all sides). Each individual service within this selector follows the airy rhythm of the mockup’s audio players: full-width interactive rows with consistent vertical spacing (8–16px) between them. A vertical gap of roughly 24–32px separates this selector from the acting-service-selector beneath, keeping the column visually segmented while maintaining an easy top-to-bottom scanning flow. On mobile, this selector appears first among the service selectors in the single-column stack, directly beneath the duration summary and budgeting area (if those are stacked).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the two-column grid, the voiceover selector participates in a standard stacked flow inside the right column. It grows vertically with content and scrolls normally. Below a responsive breakpoint (~768px), the grid collapses into a single column, and this selector becomes a full-width block sitting beneath the summary and notes sections, maintaining the same internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The acting-service-selector sits directly below the voiceover-service-selector in the right column, following the same width and alignment. It starts after a consistent vertical gap of about 24–32px, creating a clear but gentle separation between voiceover and acting offerings. It also occupies the full width of the right column (approximately 60% of the central content width) and uses similar card styling: a lightly outlined or subtly elevated panel with 16–24px internal padding, and pill-style service options stacked vertically. The goal is for the user’s eye to move effortlessly from the voiceover options down into the acting options as if scanning a continuation of the same vertical list, much like progressing through a list of audio tracks in the mockup. Another 24–32px gap below this component prepares space for the other-service-selector. On mobile, it appears directly beneath the voiceover selector in the single-column service list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The acting-service-selector is a normal, scrollable block within the right column stack. It maintains consistent spacing relative to the selectors above and below it. When the layout collapses to one column on smaller screens, it becomes a full-width block that remains in sequence after the voiceover-service-selector.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The other-service-selector is the third and final selector in the right column, positioned directly below the acting-service-selector with a consistent vertical gap of 24–32px. It mirrors the same width, alignment, and visual language: full-width within the right column, card-like container, 16–24px internal padding, and pill-style rows for each additional service type. This consistent pattern reinforces the vertical list scanning behavior established by the mockup’s audio player stack. The bottom of this selector has a comfortable 32–48px margin before the end of the main content area or before any subsequent section, ensuring the page doesn’t feel cramped at the bottom. On narrower screens, it continues the stacked sequence of service selectors in the single-column layout, appearing after acting-service-selector.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: As the last item in the right column stack, this selector scrolls in the normal document flow and expands vertically with its contents. In responsive mode, it becomes a full-width block in the single-column layout, following the voiceover and acting selectors while maintaining identical spacing rules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The duration-summary-indicator anchors the top of the left column in the main two-column grid and visually plays the role that the large portrait image played in the mockup. It occupies roughly 40% of the central content width and is positioned to the immediate left of the right-column service selectors, starting at the same top baseline as the voiceover-service-selector. This component is presented as a tall, visually distinct card with rounded corners and strong contrast (for example, a dark or slightly tinted background against the white page) so it stands out as the primary reference panel. Internally, it has generous padding (24–32px) and clearly organized metrics (total session duration, recommended max, and perhaps a simple visual meter). Vertically, it is sized to be prominent without overwhelming—roughly 50–70% of the initial viewport height—so that it remains mostly visible while the user scrolls through the right-column selectors.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the left column, the duration-summary-indicator is configured to be sticky: it remains pinned a short distance (around 80–96px) below the fixed global header as the user scrolls, ensuring the duration context is always visible while selecting services on the right. It participates in the two-column grid at desktop widths; on smaller viewports (e.g., under 768px), the sticky behavior can relax to a standard block, and the component stacks above the service selectors and budget-and-notes-section in a single-column flow, maintaining a generous margin beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-and-notes-section is positioned in the left column beneath the duration-summary-indicator, creating a supporting panel that sits in the lower half of that column. On load, it begins just below the summary card, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans the full width of the left column (about 40% of the central content width) and is styled as a lighter, more open form section with input fields for budget, priority notes, and follow-up information. Internal padding is generous (16–24px), and individual form controls are spaced by 12–16px vertically, keeping the area calm and approachable. While the user’s primary interactive scanning happens in the right column, this section remains easy to glance back to, sitting visually anchored under the more dominant summary indicator. At the bottom of this section, there is 32–48px of whitespace before the page’s bottom edge or any footer elements, preventing visual crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In the two-column layout, the budget-and-notes-section scrolls normally within the left column. If the duration-summary-indicator is sticky, this section will move behind or below it as the user scrolls past its initial vertical space, effectively making the summary feel fixed while notes move. On smaller screens, when the layout collapses to a single column, this section typically appears directly beneath the duration-summary-indicator (and above or below the service selectors depending on the intended flow), but still follows the same spacious spacing rules and full-width behavior.

name: About
title: About
description: Provides dynamically loaded background information on the talent’s voiceover and acting experience, studio capabilities, FAQs, and representation, with contextual navigation to related pages.

core_features: Load and display About page content (bio, studio setup, FAQs, representation) using the getAboutPageContent interface on initial page load.
Render each returned HTML block (bio_html, studio_setup_html, faqs_html, representation_html) into distinct, identifiable sections for in-page navigation and deep linking.
Support URL-driven section focus via a query parameter (e.g., about.html?section=bio|studio|faq|representation) to scroll to and highlight a specific section.
Provide in-page navigation controls that allow users to jump between sections and update the URL query parameter without reloading core content.
Expose contextual links from the About content to the Voiceover, Acting, Rates & Quotes, and Contact / Booking pages, attaching the required URL parameters (e.g., voiceover.html?tab=overview).

Handle link clicks embedded inside the dynamic HTML (from getAboutPageContent) so that specified triggers (e.g., 'Explore voiceover work') navigate to the correct target pages with the correct query parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: First-time visitor reads About
1. User navigates to about.html (no query parameters).
2. The about-page-loader component calls getAboutPageContent.
3. After a successful response, the loader passes bio_html, studio_setup_html, faqs_html, and representation_html to their respective section components.
4. The about-section-router reads the URL query parameter section (if present) or defaults to a preconfigured section key.
5. The router scrolls the viewport to the selected section and sets it as active (for example, via a CSS class or ARIA attribute) without requesting data again.
6. The user reads the About content, optionally using the in-page navigation to jump between sections; each navigation action updates the section query parameter in the URL.

Workflow 2: Visitor arrives with a direct link to a specific section
1. User opens a link such as about.html?section=studio from an external site or another page on the same site.
2. The about-page-loader calls getAboutPageContent once and distributes the returned HTML to all section components.
3. The about-section-router detects section=studio from the URL and programmatically scrolls to the studio section and marks it active.
4. If the user clicks another in-page navigation item (e.g., FAQs), the router scrolls to the FAQ section and updates the URL to about.html?section=faq, preserving the loaded content in memory.

Workflow 3: Visitor moves from About to Voiceover or Acting
1. User is reading either the bio section or another section where the dynamic HTML includes a link labeled 'Explore voiceover work' or 'Explore on-camera acting work'.
2. The user clicks the 'Explore voiceover work' link.
3. The about-cross-links handler prevents any unnecessary reload of About content and navigates the browser to voiceover.html?tab=overview.
4. Alternatively, when the user clicks 'Explore on-camera acting work', the handler navigates to acting.html?tab=overview.
5. Navigation is handled via standard link behavior or programmatic redirection, with no additional data requests from About.

Workflow 4: Visitor moves from About to Rates & Quotes
1. User scrolls to the studio or FAQ section (either manually or via a section link).
2. Within the content, the user sees a link labeled 'View current rates & quotes'.
3. On click, the about-cross-links component routes the user to rates.html.
4. The user continues rate/quote-related actions on the Rates page; the About page does not maintain any additional session state beyond the URL.

Workflow 5: Visitor moves from About to Contact / Booking
1. After reviewing bio, studio setup, FAQs, and representation, the user sees a call-to-action link or button in the About content labeled 'Get in touch'.
2. The user clicks this call-to-action.
3. The about-cross-links component navigates to contact_booking.html?tab=contact.
4. The Contact / Booking page uses its own logic to display the contact form, with no further interaction required from the About page.
interactions: On initial page load of about.html (with or without query parameters), the about-page-loader automatically calls getAboutPageContent and hydrates all sections with the returned HTML blocks.

When the URL query parameter section changes (due to direct navigation, browser back/forward, or in-page navigation), the about-section-router updates the active section state and scrolls to the corresponding section anchor.

When a user clicks an in-page navigation item (e.g., a list of section names at the top of the page), the click handler updates the section parameter in the URL via the History API (pushState/replaceState), then scrolls to the targeted section without refetching data.
When a user clicks the 'Explore voiceover work' link within any About section, the click handler navigates to voiceover.html with the query parameter tab=overview.
When a user clicks the 'Explore on-camera acting work' link within any About section, the click handler navigates to acting.html with the query parameter tab=overview.
When a user clicks the 'View current rates & quotes' link within the studio or FAQ content, the click handler navigates to rates.html.
When a user clicks the 'Get in touch' call-to-action within the About content, the click handler navigates to contact_booking.html with the query parameter tab=contact.

If getAboutPageContent fails (e.g., network error), the about-page-loader can show a functional error state with a retry control; when the user clicks the retry control, the loader calls getAboutPageContent again.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and the data returned from getAboutPageContent. The primary query parameter is `section`, which determines the focused section on load and during navigation. Valid values are implementation-defined but should map consistently to the About sections (for example, `bio`, `studio`, `faq`, and `representation`). On initial load: 1) the page reads `window.location.search` and parses the `section` parameter; 2) it requests content once via getAboutPageContent; 3) once content is available, it scrolls to and highlights the section matching `section`. If `section` is not present or does not match a known section, a default section key is used. In-page navigation clicks update the `section` query parameter using the History API without reloading the document, and the section-router responds to this change. Outgoing navigation from this page uses URL parameters to inform target pages of desired tabs or views: `voiceover.html?tab=overview` for voiceover, `acting.html?tab=overview` for acting, and `contact_booking.html?tab=contact` for the contact view. The About page does not store any state in localStorage or other client-side persistence; all navigable state is derived from the URL and the latest response from getAboutPageContent.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page by calling getAboutPageContent once, handling loading and error states, and distributing the returned HTML fields to the respective section components.

Manages section-level navigation based on the `section` URL parameter, including determining the active section on load, listening for URL changes (back/forward), and scrolling to the appropriate section anchors.
Displays the bio content for voiceover and acting using the HTML block provided by getAboutPageContent, and acts as a scroll target associated with the `section` key for the bio.

Displays studio setup, equipment, remote connection options, and workflow information using the HTML provided by getAboutPageContent, and acts as the scroll target for the studio-related `section` value.

Displays frequently asked questions regarding availability, turnaround time, revisions, and preparation using the HTML returned by getAboutPageContent, and acts as the scroll target for the FAQ `section` value.
Displays representation-related information using the HTML provided by getAboutPageContent and acts as the scroll target for the representation `section` value.

Provides a set of clickable controls for switching between the bio, studio, FAQ, and representation sections, updating the URL `section` query parameter and delegating scrolling behavior to the section router.

Handles clicks on contextual navigation links embedded within the dynamic About content, routing users to the appropriate pages with the required query parameters (voiceover.html?tab=overview, acting.html?tab=overview, rates.html, contact_booking.html?tab=contact).
Displays a functional error message and retry control if getAboutPageContent fails, and hides itself when content loads successfully.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 2-column layout likely based on a centered max-width container (approx. 1080–1200px) with a simple percentage-based grid. Overall layout could easily map to a 12-column system (image ~5 columns, audio list ~7 columns).
layout.layout_pattern: The About page follows the same linear top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global full-width black header at the very top → a centered, spacious page title band ('About') on white → a main two-column content band centered in a max-width container. In this band, the left column is the primary reading area where the active About section (bio, studio, FAQ, representation) is displayed; the right column is a narrower, vertically stacked in-page navigation that visually echoes the pill-like audio player list from the mockup. Below this main band, a full-width but still centered cross-link area guides users to related pages.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page follows the same linear top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global full-width black header at the very top → a centered, spacious page title band ('About') on white → a main two-column content band centered in a max-width container. In this band, the left column is the primary reading area where the active About section (bio, studio, FAQ, representation) is displayed; the right column is a narrower, vertically stacked in-page navigation that visually echoes the pill-like audio player list from the mockup. Below this main band, a full-width but still centered cross-link area guides users to related pages.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly 12–15% of viewport height. The 'About' title band below uses about 20–25% of the remaining height, with generous top and bottom margins (32–64px) to keep the layout airy. The main two-column content band uses the rest of the viewport height on first load. Horizontally, the central content is confined to a 1080–1200px max-width container with roughly 15–20% of the viewport width combined as side margins. Within that, the left content column takes about 60–65% of the width and the right in-page navigation column takes about 35–40%. The cross-links band at the bottom uses similar margins and spacing, separated from the main content by a 48–64px vertical gap.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious. Each major band (title, two-column content, cross-links) is separated by 32–64px vertical whitespace. Within the two-column band, the active section content is grouped into distinct subsections with clear headings and 24–32px spacing between blocks. Navigation items on the right are pill-shaped buttons with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, stacked with consistent 16–24px gaps, resulting in a clean, legible vertical rhythm similar to the audio players in the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the mockup’s Z-pattern: the user scans the black header from left to right, then drops to the centered 'About' title. From there, the eye moves down and slightly left into the main content column where the currently active section begins (mirroring the left hero image in the mockup), then shifts horizontally to the right to the stacked in-page navigation links. Users then scan down this right column stack to understand available sections and jump between them. Within the main column, scrolling continues vertically, with the navigation column either staying visible (sticky) or following, reinforcing the vertical scanning flow established by the audio players in the original design.

layout.overall_description: On the About page, the familiar full-width black header with logo and navigation anchors the top of the screen. Below it, on a white background, a large, elegant 'About' heading sits centered in a wide band of whitespace, echoing the prominence and breathing room of the 'Acting' title in the mockup. Under this, the layout resolves into a centered two-column section within a max-width container: on the left, a broad, white content column holds whichever About subsection is currently active (bio, studio setup, FAQs, or representation), presented in clean, stacked blocks; on the right, a narrower column houses a vertical stack of pill-shaped navigation items representing those same subsections. These pills echo the audio-player styling from the original design, becoming the main interactive focus on this page. As users scroll, the left column’s content flows down the page while the right navigation remains visually cohesive and easily reachable, guiding jumps between sections. At the bottom of the main content, a final full-width but centered row of prominent cross-links to related pages appears, framed by generous vertical padding, giving the page a clear, theatrical yet minimal composition that matches the rest of the portfolio.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The async loader is positioned within the left main content column, occupying the same horizontal space as the active About section. When active, it overlays or replaces the section area starting just below the bottom edge of the 'About' title band. It spans the full width of the left column (about 60–65% of the content container), centered horizontally within the page, and extends down enough to visually fill the typical height of the first screenful of content (roughly 40–60% of the viewport height). Vertically, it is separated from the 'About' heading above by a 32–48px gap, mirroring the spacing used between the title and the main two-column band. The loader can present as a subtle card or skeleton blocks with 16–24px internal padding, maintaining the clean, minimalist aesthetic and ensuring it feels like a natural part of the main content flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When loading, the loader temporarily replaces or overlays the active section area in the left column, effectively blocking interaction with section content while leaving the header and right in-page navigation visible. It is not fixed to the viewport but scrolls with the page content; once data is loaded, it collapses to zero height and yields space back to the section content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL-based section router is an invisible structural layer that controls which of the About subsections is rendered in the left main content column. It sits directly beneath the 'About' heading band and the initial vertical gap, and it is constrained to the left column of the main two-column layout. In practical terms, the router defines a vertical stack region that occupies roughly 60–65% of the central content width and extends down with the height of whichever section is currently active. Its top aligns horizontally with the top of the right in-page navigation column, ensuring the start of the main content and the first navigation pill sit on the same horizontal baseline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The router itself does not render visible UI; it behaves as a static container within the normal document flow. It swaps the visible child section (bio, studio setup, FAQ, representation) based on URL state or in-page navigation interactions, without affecting the positioning of the right-hand navigation or the surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the bio section fills the upper portion of the left main content column, becoming the first substantial block the eye encounters after the 'About' heading. It sits 32–48px below the title band and aligns to the left edge of the main content column, spanning its full width (about 60–65% of the max-width container). Inside this section, content is arranged vertically in generous blocks: a leading headline or subheading at the top, followed by paragraphs of copy and potentially a portrait-style image or pull-quote aligned within this column. Each internal block is separated by 24–32px of whitespace to keep the narrative airy and readable. The right in-page navigation column begins at the same vertical starting line as the top of the bio section, so as users start reading the bio on the left, they can glance right to see the stacked navigation pills.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The bio section is part of the standard scroll flow within the left column. It expands vertically with its content and pushes subsequent sections down; only one primary About section (bio, studio setup, FAQ, or representation) is visible within the router area at a time. On smaller screens, when the layout collapses to a single column, the bio section appears directly beneath the in-page navigation or title according to responsive rules, still preserving comfortable spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The studio setup section occupies the same spatial footprint as the bio section when it is active in the router: it fills the left main content column, beginning at the same horizontal alignment and stack position under the 'About' heading. It spans the full width of this column, matching the 60–65% content width and employing 16–24px internal padding within its cards or sub-blocks. Information about equipment, recording environment, and technical specs is arranged in vertically stacked segments or optional nested columns that never exceed the boundaries of the left column. Each segment is separated by 24–32px vertical gaps for clarity, echoing the spaced-out feel of the audio tracks in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Like the other content sections, the studio setup section is a scrollable block within the left column and appears only when routed to. It pushes content below it further down the page but does not affect the fixed positioning of the header. On narrow viewports, it reflows into a full-width block beneath the navigation, maintaining 32–48px spacing above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ section, when active, also resides in the left main content column, occupying the same width and alignment as the other sections. It begins at the same vertical offset from the bottom of the 'About' title band and is horizontally centered within the overall container. Questions are organized into a vertical list of expandable rows or cards, each running nearly the full width of the column, with 8–16px vertical padding inside and 16–24px between rows. This creates a visually similar rhythm to the stacked audio players, but within the left content column: user focus moves down the list in a clear, linear manner, while the right column shows the navigation stack in parallel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The FAQ section scrolls within the normal document flow and only appears when the router directs to it. If questions exceed the initial viewport height, the page scrolls, while the right-hand navigation (on desktop) can remain sticky alongside, so users can jump to other sections without scrolling back to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The representation section appears in the same left column region controlled by the router, corresponding exactly to the spatial zone used by the bio, studio setup, and FAQ sections. It spans the full width of the left column and is aligned flush with its left and right edges. This section is likely more compact, featuring key contact details, agency or manager information, and possibly a concise call-to-action, arranged in 1–3 vertical content blocks with 24–32px gaps between them. It begins 32–48px below the 'About' title band and finishes with an additional 32–48px gap beneath it before any cross-link content appears, preserving a clear separation from the next band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The representation section participates in the same scroll behavior as the other sections and is only visible when routed to. On smaller viewports, it expands to full width, with its content stacked neatly and generously spaced so that key information is easily tap-targetable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page navigation column is positioned to the right of the main content column, starting at the same vertical height as the top of the active section in the left column. It occupies approximately 35–40% of the central content width and is aligned flush to the right inside the max-width container, leaving equal or slightly larger white margins on the outer right side of the page. Within this column, navigation items for 'Bio', 'Studio Setup', 'FAQ', and 'Representation' are arranged as pill-shaped buttons or minimal cards, each spanning nearly the full width of this right column. Every pill has 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, rounded corners, and subtle borders or shadows, echoing the pill-style audio players. They are stacked vertically with consistent 16–24px gaps between them, inviting a top-to-bottom scanning pattern. A small label or subtext under each title can provide short descriptions, mirroring the audio track labels seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the in-page navigation column is sticky relative to the viewport once scrolled past the 'About' heading, remaining visible along the right side as the left column content scrolls. This maintains a strong vertical navigation presence, similar to the fixed perception of the audio-player stack. On smaller screens, the navigation reflows above the content as a horizontal scrollable bar or a stacked list that sits directly under the 'About' heading, no longer sticky but still separated from the content by a 16–24px gap.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cross-links section is placed below the main two-column band, serving as a footer-like content strip within the same central max-width container. It starts after a generous 48–64px vertical gap beneath the end of whichever About section is active. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the container (80–90% of the central content band), centered, with equal padding on left and right to align with the columns above. Inside, related page links (e.g., to the 'Acting' page, 'Voiceover Demos', or 'Contact') are presented as a row or grid of 2–4 pill-style or card-style buttons, each with ample 12–16px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding. The spacing between these cross-link items is consistent (16–24px), and the entire block is framed with 24–32px top and bottom internal padding to visually separate it from the main About content while still feeling like part of the same design system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The cross-links section is a static, full-width band at the bottom of the About page content. It scrolls naturally into view as users reach the end of the active section and does not exhibit sticky or floating behavior. On narrow screens, its items stack vertically with maintained spacing, preserving tap-friendly targets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state appears in the same spatial area as the main content sections within the left column, effectively replacing the section content when loading fails or data cannot be retrieved. It starts at the usual 32–48px gap below the 'About' heading and spans the full width of the left column (60–65% of the container), aligned with the right-hand navigation column. The error message is presented inside a clearly defined card or panel with 16–24px internal padding, subtle border or background tint against white, and a short, direct explanation along with an action button (e.g., 'Retry') centered or left-aligned. Vertical whitespace of 24–32px separates the message text from the action button, and 32–48px of whitespace appear below the error card before the page footer or cross-links, preserving the page’s airy structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The error state component replaces the usual routed section content inside the router’s region without altering the layout of the in-page navigation or the cross-links section. It scrolls with the page like any other content. If the user retries and loading succeeds, the error card collapses and the appropriate section content reappears in its place.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: This page retrieves and displays the latest privacy policy content, including the last-updated date, and provides deep links to specific sections and a dedicated action to contact the site owner about privacy matters.

core_features: Fetch the current privacy policy content and last-updated date via getPrivacyPolicyContent and render it on page load.
Display the policy’s last-updated date in a clearly identifiable utility area of the page.
Render the HTML policy body returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent.content_html, including any headings, lists, and internal anchors.
Support deep-linking to specific sections of the policy using a URL parameter (e.g., privacy.html?section=some-section-id) that scrolls to and optionally highlights the matching section element.
Provide a dedicated "Contact us about privacy" interaction within the policy content that navigates to contact_booking.html with the URL parameter tab=contact.
Offer a print-friendly action that triggers the browser’s print dialog, optionally controlled via a URL parameter (e.g., privacy.html?mode=print) for direct print links.
Allow optional manual refresh of the privacy policy content (e.g., after a long-open tab) by re-calling getPrivacyPolicyContent without reloading the entire page.
user_workflows: View the privacy policy: The user clicks the footer "Privacy Policy" link or opens privacy.html directly. On page load, the page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent, displays the last-updated date in a header/toolbar area, and renders the returned content_html as the main body.

Open a specific policy section from a shared link: The user opens a URL such as privacy.html?section=some-section-id. After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves and the content is rendered, the page reads the section URL parameter, finds the element with a matching id in the policy content, scrolls it into view, and optionally highlights it.

Contact about privacy: While reading the policy, the user clicks the "Contact us about privacy" link embedded within the policy text. The click handler intercepts the click and navigates the browser to contact_booking.html?tab=contact so the user can send a privacy-related message via the contact form.

Print the privacy policy: The user clicks a print action in the page toolbar, or opens a URL such as privacy.html?mode=print. The page, after loading and rendering the policy, triggers window.print() so the user can print or save the policy as PDF.

Manually refresh policy content: The user clicks a “refresh” action in the toolbar (or similar control). The page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent again, updates the last-updated date and content_html in the view, and re-applies any URL-based section navigation or highlighting.
interactions: Page load: On initial load of privacy.html, an initialization handler invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent. While waiting, a loading state is shown; when the call succeeds, last_updated and content_html are stored in component state and rendered; on failure, an error message is shown with an option to retry.
Footer navigation: Clicking the global footer link labeled "Privacy Policy" changes window.location to privacy.html (without additional parameters), which triggers the normal page load behavior.

Deep-link navigation via URL parameter: When the page loads or when the URL changes (e.g., via history API), the component responsible for section navigation reads the current section parameter. If present, after content_html has been inserted into the DOM, it scrolls the element with id equal to the parameter value into view and applies a temporary visual highlight. If no matching element exists, no scrolling occurs.

"Contact us about privacy" link click: A delegated click handler on the rendered policy content detects clicks on elements marked in content_html as the privacy-contact link (for example, via a data attribute). The handler prevents the default in-page navigation (if any) and instead navigates to contact_booking.html?tab=contact.

Print button click: Clicking the print control in the toolbar triggers window.print(). If the mode URL parameter is not already set to print, the handler may optionally push a new history state with mode=print so that reloading the page keeps print-friendly context.

Manual refresh click: Clicking the refresh control triggers a new call to getPrivacyPolicyContent. During the call, the content area may show a secondary loading state. When the new content arrives, last_updated and content_html in the view are replaced, and section deep-link logic is re-run using the current URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page does not require any URL parameters to function, but it reacts to optional parameters for view state and navigation. The `section` parameter (e.g., privacy.html?section=some-section-id) is read on page load and on history changes; once getPrivacyPolicyContent has completed and the HTML content is rendered, the page searches the DOM for an element whose id matches the `section` value. If found, it scrolls to that element and may apply a transient highlight; if not found, the page remains at the default scroll position. The `mode` parameter can be used to control view mode; for example, if `mode=print` is present when the page finishes rendering content, the page automatically calls window.print() and may adjust minor layout classes for print-friendliness. Any additional URL parameters are ignored by this page’s functional logic. When navigating to contact_booking.html via the privacy contact link, the page always appends `tab=contact` to the destination URL to ensure the contact tab is active there.

layout.layout_pattern: The page maintains the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width black header bar fixed/anchored at the top, followed by a generous vertical gap, then a centered main content area on a white background. The main area opens with a large, centered page title ('Privacy Policy') and a small subline for the last-updated date. Below this heading block, the page transitions into a two-column layout: the left column acts as a functional sidebar with deep links to policy sections and a dedicated 'privacy contact' action, while the right column holds the scrolling privacy policy text. All content sits in a max-width centered container to preserve the same banded, theater-like stage feeling of the original design.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page maintains the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width black header bar fixed/anchored at the top, followed by a generous vertical gap, then a centered main content area on a white background. The main area opens with a large, centered page title ('Privacy Policy') and a small subline for the last-updated date. layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the black header occupies roughly 12–15% of the viewport height. Beneath it, a 32–48px gap separates the header from the main content. The title/metadata block (page title + last-updated info + optional short intro) uses about 20–25% of the remaining visible height with generous top and bottom margins (32–64px total) to echo the breathing room around the 'Acting' heading in the mockup. The remaining vertical space is dedicated to the two-column content area. Within this container, the left column (section links and contact card) occupies roughly 30–35% of the content width, while the right column (policy text) occupies the remaining 65–70%, mirroring the relative emphasis previously given to the audio list.
